message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

read /optimize skill about the 'analyze' state.
how to identify the active path?  Why do we identify from the assembly of objdump?

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	---
2	name: optimize
3	description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
4	argument-hint: [function_name|status|reset]
5	disable-model-invocation: false
6	allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
7	---
8	
9	# libnn RVV Optimization Skill
10	
11	Fully autonomous optimization loop. State persisted in `.claude/opt-state.json`.
12	
13	## Current State
14	
15	!`LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel); cat ${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/opt-state.json 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`
16	
17	## Commands
18	
19	- `/optimize <function_name>` — Start optimizing a function (loops until flat land).
20	- `/optimize status` — Show current state
21	- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle
22	
23	Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`
24	
25	## Finite State Machine
26	
27	```
28	idle → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
29	                  ↑ |          ↑          |                             |
30	                  | |          └──────────┘                             |
31	                  | |       (verify fail → fix → retry)                 |
32	                  | └→ idle (no opportunity found)                       |
33	                  |                                                      |
34	                  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
35	                    (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)
36	
37	Exit conditions:
38	  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
39	  - max 10 rounds per function → idle
40	```
41	
42	The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from FPGA measurements, updated assembly, and recomputed metrics. The verify/measure loop provides real feedback.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## Build Scripts Reference
47	
48	All scripts are at `qemu-validate/` (relative to project root). Always use `/usr/bin/bash` to invoke them. In every bash block, define: `LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)`
49	
50	| Script | Purpose | Time |
51	|--------|---------|------|
52	| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files). Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
53	| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
54	| qemu-runner subagent | QEMU correctness test (handles fd 9 semihosting). Compile test binary first, then dispatch agent. | ~1s |
55	| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. | ~30s |
56	
57	---
58	
59	## State: `idle`
60	
61	No optimization in progress.
62	
63	If a function name is provided, transition → `select`.
64	If "status", report idle. If "reset", clear state.
65	
66	---
67	
68	## State: `select`
69	
70	Validate the function and gather baseline context.
71	
72	1. Find the source file under `Source/` — grep for the function name
73	2. Confirm it has `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` (existing RVV to improve)
74	3. Find corresponding test in `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`
75	4. Query the wiki for prior lessons and applicable techniques:
76	   - Read `docs/wiki/index.md` to identify relevant pages
77	   - Read the operator page in `docs/wiki/operators/` if it exists (e.g., `conv-hwc-s8-sym-bias-fast.md`)
78	   - Read matching `docs/wiki/general/` pages for applicable techniques and known anti-patterns
79	5. Full build (first time only):
80	   ```bash
81	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/build_libnn.sh
82	   ```
83	6. Run baseline FPGA measurement (**MUST use this exact script — do NOT compile or run FPGA tests manually**):
84	   ```bash
85	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
86	   ```
87	   Parse cycles from the output line: `RESULT: PASS (cycles=NNNN)`
88	7. **Identify the active code path** from the linked test binary (produced by step 6):
89	   ```bash
90	   # Find all function calls from the target function in the linked binary
91	   grep -A500 '<function_name>:' qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | \
92	     grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" | head -20
93	   ```
94	   Record which sub-functions are called (e.g., GEMM kernel name). This is ground truth for which `#ifdef` path was compiled. Save in state as `called_functions`.
95	8. Save state: function, source_file, test_name, cycles_before, instrs_before, called_functions.
96	
97	**Transition:** → `analyze`
98	**Fail:** Function not found or no RVV → stay `select`, report error.
99	
100	---
101	
102	## State: `analyze`
103	
104	Study the current RVV code and assembly to form the next optimization hypothesis.
105	
106	**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from the COMPILED BINARY, not from source `#ifdef` guards.**
107	
108	Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from source alone.** Instead:
109	
110	1. Check the linked test binary's objdump for what functions are actually called:
111	   ```bash
112	   # Look at the perf objdump from the select step's FPGA run
113	   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
114	   ```
115	2. Cross-reference the called sub-functions (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) back to the source to identify which `#if/#elif` block is active.
116	3. Read only the ACTIVE block. Modifying dead code has zero effect.
117	
118	**Key defines active for AX45MPV** (from build.sh + internal headers):
119	- `ENA_VEC_ISA` — from build.sh
120	- `NDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000` — from build.sh
121	- `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD` — from build.sh
122	- `ENA_TILING` — from `internal/internal_nn_math.h:2256`
123	- `ENA_FAST_ALGO` — from `internal/internal_nn_math.h`
124	- `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` — **NOT defined** (dead code)
125	- `ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC` — **NOT defined** (unless toolchain provides it implicitly for some files)
126	
127	### Analysis
128	
129	**On first round (round=1):**
130	1. Identify the active code path using the linked binary's objdump. Read that specific block in the source.
131	2. Find the function's assembly in `libnn.objdump`:
132	   ```bash
133	   grep -A 200 '<function_name>:' ${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump | head -300
134	   ```
135	3. If the function calls sub-functions (GEMM kernels, etc.), also read those sub-functions' assembly and source
136	4. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md`, relevant `general/` pages for similar patterns, operator page if it exists
137	5. Compute key metrics from the assembly and source:
138	   - **Arithmetic Intensity** = FLOPs per byte transferred from memory. Count MAC-equivalent ops (e.g., each `vqmacc` contributes `2 * vl` FLOPs for LMUL-dependent `vl`); count bytes loaded/stored per loop iteration (load instructions x element width x vl). AI = FLOPs / bytes.
139	   - **Reuse Rate** = FLOPs per byte loaded (exclude stores). Measures how many times each loaded byte contributes to computation before being evicted. High reuse = cache-friendly; low reuse = memory-bound.
140	   - Save both as `arith_intensity` and `reuse_rate` in state.
141	6. Identify bottleneck using the metrics: low AI -> memory-bound (improve tiling/blocking/reuse); high AI but low IPC -> compute-bound (LMUL, register pressure, loop structure)
142	7. Form hypothesis: what to change and expected impact
143	
144	**On subsequent rounds (round>1):**
145	1. Re-read the source (it changed last round)
146	2. Re-check the objdump for the updated assembly
147	3. Recompute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the updated assembly. Compare to previous round — did the change improve reuse or reduce memory traffic?
148	4. Review FPGA cycle/instruction delta from last round's `measure` step to identify new bottleneck
149	5. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant `general/` pages for the specific bottleneck pattern
150	6. Form the next hypothesis based on what the metrics reveal
151	
152	**Proposer output:** 1–3 ranked ideas, each with what to change, which lines, expected impact rationale, and risk assessment. Include current arithmetic intensity and reuse rate in the summary. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.
153	
154	### Output and Transitions
155	
156	**Output:** Print the analysis summary (including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, and hypothesis). Do NOT wait for approval — proceed immediately.
157	
158	**Decision gate:** If no concrete optimization opportunity can be identified (assembly looks clean, metrics are already optimal, LMUL/tiling are already well-tuned):
159	- Transition to idle with final summary.
160	- Do NOT force an optimization that has no clear rationale.
161	
162	**Transition:** → `implement` (if opportunity found) or → `idle` (if no opportunity)
163	
164	---
165	
166	## State: `implement`
167	
168	Apply the optimization.
169	
170	1. Modify ONLY the active code path identified during `analyze`. The active path was determined by cross-referencing the linked binary's function calls with source `#ifdef` blocks. **Do NOT guess from `#ifdef` guards alone** — many macros (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO`) are defined in internal headers, not build.sh.
171	2. If the function calls sub-functions (e.g., a GEMM kernel), the optimization may need to be applied in the sub-function's source file, not the conv function's source.
172	3. Do NOT touch the plain C fallback or dead `#ifdef` blocks
173	4. Save a description of what was changed in state (`changes` field)
174	5. If this is round>1 and the previous round regressed, revert the regression first, then try the new approach
175	
176	**CRITICAL — Intrinsic API selection:**
177	The active `ENA_VEC_ISA` code path uses **`NDS_VEC_*` macros** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`, `NDS_VEC_VNCLIP_WX`). These are defined in `internal/internal_vec_isa_v1_0.h`.
178	
179	**NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics** (e.g., `__riscv_vle8_v_i8m2`, `__riscv_vqmacc_vx_i32m8`) in `ENA_VEC_ISA` blocks. Those require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is **NOT defined** for AX45MPV builds. Using them causes compilation failures.
180	
181	When writing new RVV code, always:
182	- Study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef` block to learn the available `NDS_VEC_*` macro names
183	- Use explicit register names (`NDS_VEC_V0`, `NDS_VEC_V8`, `NDS_VEC_V16`, etc.)
184	- Match the LMUL register grouping: M8 uses v0/v8/v16/v24, M4 uses v0/v4/v8/.., M2 uses v0/v2/v4/..
185	- For loads: `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VLE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VLE32_V` (e32)
186	- For stores: `NDS_VEC_VSE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VSE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VSE32_V` (e32)
187	
188	**Transition:** → `verify`
189	
190	---
191	
192	## State: `verify`
193	
194	Incremental rebuild and QEMU correctness test.
195	
196	1. Rebuild ONLY the modified source file (fast incremental):
197	   ```bash
198	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh <source_file>
199	   ```
200	   Example:
201	   ```bash
202	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
203	   ```
204	
205	2. Compile the test binary (compile-only, no QEMU execution):
206	   ```bash
207	   TOOLCHAIN_BIN="/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
208	   export PATH="${TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${PATH}"
209	   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
210	   UNIT_TEST_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/Examples/unit_func"
211	   WORK_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/work"
212	   mkdir -p "${WORK_DIR}"
213	   riscv64-elf-gcc -O3 -Wall -Werror -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -Wl,-gc-sections \
214	     -mvh -mzfh -DENA_FS_VS \
215	     -mtune=andes-45-series -mext-vector=zve64d -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 \
216	     -DENA_FAST_ALGO -DENA_DUMP_GOLDEN=0 -DENA_MEASURE_PERF=0 \
217	     -DGETENV_TESTDIR=${UNIT_TEST_DIR} \
218	     -I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${UNIT_TEST_DIR}/include \
219	     -o "${WORK_DIR}/t_<test_name>.adx" \
220	     "${UNIT_TEST_DIR}/t_<test_name>.c" "${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.a" -lm
221	   ```
222	
223	3. Run the compiled ELF via the **qemu-runner subagent** (handles semihosting fd 9 capture):
224	   ```
225	   Agent(subagent_type="qemu-runner", prompt="Run ELF: ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/work/t_<test_name>.adx at VLEN=1024 with flags: --cwd ${LIB_ROOT}/Examples/unit_func")
226	   ```
227	   Parse the output for "accuracy checking pass" / "accuracy checking fail".
228	
229	4. Do NOT run full regression in the optimization loop — only run the target function's test. Full regression is too slow for iterative optimization.
230	
231	**Pass:** → `measure`
232	**Compile error:** Launch a subagent to summarize the compilation error (what failed, which macro/function/type is missing, the exact error message). Then launch a NEW subagent with the summary to fix the code. Rebuild and re-verify. Max 3 retries before going back to `analyze` with a different approach.
233	**Test fail (compiled but wrong results):** → `implement` (analyze the failure, fix, retry. Max 3 retries before going back to `analyze` with a different approach)
234	
235	---
236	
237	## State: `measure`
238	
239	Run on the AX45MPV FPGA board via GDB for ground-truth cycle counts.
240	
241	```bash
242	/usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
243	```
244	
245	This script:
246	1. Compiles the test with `-DPF_COUNTER -DENA_TEST_PERF -DENA_RUN_TWICE` flags
247	2. Uploads binary to FPGA via GDB expect script (`riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board`)
248	3. Runs on AX45MPV board (`sw-boards.andestech.com:1116`)
249	4. Prints cycle count: `"The cycle count is NNNN"`
250	5. Generates `.adx` and `.objdump` in `qemu-validate/work/`
251	
252	Parse the output for:
253	- `"The cycle count is <N>"` — the FPGA cycle count
254	- `"The inst count is <N>"` — the FPGA instruction count
255	- `"accuracy checking pass/fail"` — correctness check
256	
257	Save `cycles_after` and `instrs_after` in state. Compute speedup: `cycles_before / cycles_after`. Compute IPC: `instrs_after / cycles_after`.
258	
259	**Transition:** → `learn`
260	
261	---
262	
263	## State: `learn`
264	
265	Record the lesson, then loop back.
266	
267	1. Update the wiki following the wiki-writer skill:
268	   - Read `.claude/skills/wiki-writer/SKILL.md`
269	   - Input context from state: function, source_file, round, hypothesis, changes, cycles_before, cycles_after, speedup, arith_intensity, reuse_rate, outcome
270	   - Execute all 4 operations: operator page, general page (if applicable), index, log
271	   - All content must follow the naive expression style defined in wiki-writer
272	
273	2. Increment the `round` counter in state.
274	
275	3. **Check exit condition:**
276	   - If `round >= 5`, STOP → `idle`. Print final summary of all rounds.
277	   - Otherwise, transition → `analyze` for the next optimization round on the same function.
278	
279	4. When looping back to `analyze`, key inputs for the next round are: FPGA cycle/instruction counts from `measure`, updated arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from this round, updated assembly, and wiki pages. The `analyze` step will decide whether there's still an opportunity. If not, it exits to `idle`.
280	
281	**Exit → `idle`:** Print a summary table of all rounds (round#, approach, cycles, instrs, IPC, AI, reuse_rate, speedup, insight). Instruction count diagnoses whether gains came from fewer instructions (algorithmic) or better IPC (cache/scheduling).
282	**Continue → `analyze`:** Print "Round N+1: continuing. AI=X, reuse_rate=Y, key finding: <insight>"
283	
284	---
285	
286	## State File Format
287	
288	```json
289	{
290	  "step": "idle|select|analyze|implement|verify|measure|learn",
291	  "function": "function_name",
292	  "source_file": "Source/.../file.c",
293	  "test_name": "test_name_for_qemu",
294	  "round": 0,
295	  "hypothesis": "what we plan to change and why",
296	  "changes": "description of what was implemented",
297	  "cycles_before": "",
298	  "cycles_after": "",
299	  "instrs_before": "",
300	  "instrs_after": "",
301	  "speedup": "",
302	  "arith_intensity": "",
303	  "reuse_rate": "",
304	  "rounds_without_improvement": 0,
305	  "round_history": [
306	    {
307	      "round": 1,
308	      "approach": "...",
309	      "cycles": "...",
310	      "instrs": "...",
311	      "ipc": "...",
312	      "arith_intensity": "...",
313	      "reuse_rate": "...",
314	      "speedup": "...",
315	      "outcome": "improved|regressed|neutral",
316	      "insight": "..."
317	    }
318	  ],
319	  "history": ["timestamps of state transitions"]
320	}
321	```
322	
323	Update state file on every transition:
324	```bash
325	python3 -c "
326	import json
327	from datetime import datetime
328	import subprocess
329	lib_root = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'rev-parse', '--show-toplevel']).decode().strip()
330	f = lib_root + '/.claude/opt-state.json'
331	state = json.load(open(f))
332	prev_step = state['step']
333	new_step = '<new_step>'
334	now = datetime.now()
335	state['step'] = new_step
336	state['step_started'] = now.isoformat()
337	state['history'].append(f'{now.isoformat()} {new_step}')
338	# Calculate time spent in previous step
339	if 'step_started' in state and state['history']:
340	    prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get('prev_step_started', now.isoformat()))
341	    elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
342	    state['last_step_duration'] = f'{prev_step}: {elapsed:.1f}s'
343	state['prev_step_started'] = state.get('step_started', now.isoformat())
344	json.dump(state, open(f, 'w'), indent=2)
345	print(json.dumps(state, indent=2))
346	"
347	```
348	
349	---
350	
351	## Subagent Dispatch
352	
353	The main agent drives the FSM and reports progress to the user between every step. Only steps that require reasoning use subagents. Mechanical steps run commands directly.
354	
355	| Step | Execution | Why |
356	|------|-----------|-----|
357	| **select** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just shell commands (grep, build, FPGA) |
358	| **analyze** | Subagent (proposer) | Requires reading code + assembly + metrics, forming hypothesis |
359	| **implement** | Subagent | Requires code editing with domain knowledge |
360	| **verify** | Main agent compiles test. qemu-runner subagent runs ELF. On compile error: subagent summarizes error → new subagent fixes code | Compile is mechanical; QEMU needs fd 9 handling via qemu-runner; error diagnosis and fix need reasoning |
361	| **measure** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just `run_fpga_test.sh` |
362	| **learn** | Main agent runs commands directly | Wiki page updates + state update |
363	
364	### Subagent constraints
365	
366	When dispatching to a subagent, always include these constraints in the prompt:
367	
368	**For `analyze` subagent (proposer):**
369	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (ONLY for `grep` on objdump)
370	- DO NOT edit any files
371	- DO NOT run build, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands
372	- **MUST use the linked test binary objdump** (`qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump`) to identify the active code path by checking which sub-functions are called (e.g., `jal` targets). Cross-reference with source `#ifdef` blocks. Never guess from source alone — `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` etc. are defined in internal headers.
373	- Focus on data flow, algorithm choice, tiling/blocking, loop structure, GEMM strategy, im2col, unrolling, LMUL selection, vector register utilization. Compute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the assembly. Must read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant wiki pages for what's been tried.
374	- Output: analysis summary including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, active code path (verified from objdump), and 1–3 ranked ideas with expected impact rationale, risk assessment, and which lines to modify
375	
376	**For `verify error-summary` subagent (on compilation failure):**
377	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob
378	- DO NOT edit any files or run any commands
379	- Input: the full compilation error output
380	- Output: concise summary — what failed, which macro/function/type is missing or misused, the exact error messages, and which source file/line caused it. This summary is passed to the fix subagent.
381	
382	**For `verify error-fix` subagent (after error-summary):**
383	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
384	- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
385	- Input: the error summary from the error-summary subagent + the source file path
386	- Fix the compilation error based on the summary. Follow the same intrinsic rules as the implement subagent (NDS_VEC_* macros only, no __riscv_* intrinsics).
387	- Output: description of exact code change made to fix the error
388	
389	**For `implement` subagent:**
390	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
391	- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
392	- DO NOT run build, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands
393	- DO NOT compile anything
394	- **CRITICAL: Use `NDS_VEC_*` macros ONLY** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`). NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics — they require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is NOT defined. Before writing new RVV code, read an existing working function in the same `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` block to learn the correct macro names.
395	- Output: description of exact code change made
396	
397	---
398	
399	## Rules
400	
401	1. **Fully autonomous.** Do not wait for user input at any step. Run the entire loop.
402	2. **Main agent drives the FSM.** The main agent owns state, runs mechanical steps directly, dispatches reasoning steps to subagents, and reports progress to the user between every step.
403	3. **Never skip steps.** Follow FSM transitions strictly.
404	4. **Max 5 rounds per function.** After round 5, exit to idle regardless.
405	5. **analyze is the gatekeeper.** If it finds no optimization opportunity from code + assembly + metrics, exit to idle. Do not force pointless changes.
406	6. **FPGA is ground truth.** QEMU is correctness only.
407	7. **Always record lessons.** Wins AND losses. This compounds knowledge across functions.
408	8. **Target VLEN=1024.** Never sacrifice VLEN=1024 performance.
409	9. **If verify fails 3 times**, go back to `analyze` with a different approach, not the same one.
410	10. **If a round regresses**, revert the code before trying the next approach.
411	11. **Arithmetic intensity and reuse rate drive the next round.** Recompute them each round from updated assembly to track whether changes improved memory efficiency.
412	12. **Use `rebuild_single.sh` for incremental builds** after the initial `build_libnn.sh` in `select`. Never rebuild the full library in the optimization loop.
413	13. **Use `run_fpga_test.sh` for FPGA measurement.** It handles compilation with perf flags, GDB upload, and cycle count parsing. **NEVER compile or run FPGA tests manually — always use this script.**
414	14. **Only test the target function** during the optimization loop. Full regression is for final validation only.
415	15. **Scripts are the single source of truth for builds and tests.** Never construct gcc, QEMU, or GDB commands manually. Always use the scripts in `qemu-validate/`.
416

file.content:
---
name: optimize
description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
argument-hint: [function_name|status|reset]
disable-model-invocation: false
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
---

# libnn RVV Optimization Skill

Fully autonomous optimization loop. State persisted in `.claude/opt-state.json`.

## Current State

!`LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel); cat ${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/opt-state.json 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`

## Commands

- `/optimize <function_name>` — Start optimizing a function (loops until flat land).
- `/optimize status` — Show current state
- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle

Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`

## Finite State Machine

```
idle → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
                  ↑ |          ↑          |                             |
                  | |          └──────────┘                             |
                  | |       (verify fail → fix → retry)                 |
                  | └→ idle (no opportunity found)                       |
                  |                                                      |
                  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)

Exit conditions:
  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
  - max 10 rounds per function → idle
```

The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from FPGA measurements, updated assembly, and recomputed metrics. The verify/measure loop provides real feedback.

---

## Build Scripts Reference

All scripts are at `qemu-validate/` (relative to project root). Always use `/usr/bin/bash` to invoke them. In every bash block, define: `LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)`

| Script | Purpose | Time |
|--------|---------|------|
| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files). Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
| qemu-runner subagent | QEMU correctness test (handles fd 9 semihosting). Compile test binary first, then dispatch agent. | ~1s |
| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. | ~30s |

---

## State: `idle`

No optimization in progress.

If a function name is provided, transition → `select`.
If "status", report idle. If "reset", clear state.

---

## State: `select`

Validate the function and gather baseline context.

1. Find the source file under `Source/` — grep for the function name
2. Confirm it has `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` (existing RVV to improve)
3. Find corresponding test in `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`
4. Query the wiki for prior lessons and applicable techniques:
   - Read `docs/wiki/index.md` to identify relevant pages
   - Read the operator page in `docs/wiki/operators/` if it exists (e.g., `conv-hwc-s8-sym-bias-fast.md`)
   - Read matching `docs/wiki/general/` pages for applicable techniques and known anti-patterns
5. Full build (first time only):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/build_libnn.sh
   ```
6. Run baseline FPGA measurement (**MUST use this exact script — do NOT compile or run FPGA tests manually**):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
   ```
   Parse cycles from the output line: `RESULT: PASS (cycles=NNNN)`
7. **Identify the active code path** from the linked test binary (produced by step 6):
   ```bash
   # Find all function calls from the target function in the linked binary
   grep -A500 '<function_name>:' qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | \
     grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" | head -20
   ```
   Record which sub-functions are called (e.g., GEMM kernel name). This is ground truth for which `#ifdef` path was compiled. Save in state as `called_functions`.
8. Save state: function, source_file, test_name, cycles_before, instrs_before, called_functions.

**Transition:** → `analyze`
**Fail:** Function not found or no RVV → stay `select`, report error.

---

## State: `analyze`

Study the current RVV code and assembly to form the next optimization hypothesis.

**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from the COMPILED BINARY, not from source `#ifdef` guards.**

Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from source alone.** Instead:

1. Check the linked test binary's objdump for what functions are actually called:
   ```bash
   # Look at the perf objdump from the select step's FPGA run
   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
   ```
2. Cross-reference the called sub-functions (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) back to the source to identify which `#if/#elif` block is active.
3. Read only the ACTIVE block. Modifying dead code has zero effect.

**Key defines active for AX45MPV** (from build.sh + internal headers):
- `ENA_VEC_ISA` — from build.sh
- `NDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000` — from build.sh
- `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD` — from build.sh
- `ENA_TILING` — from `internal/internal_nn_math.h:2256`
- `ENA_FAST_ALGO` — from `internal/internal_nn_math.h`
- `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` — **NOT defined** (dead code)
- `ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC` — **NOT defined** (unless toolchain provides it implicitly for some files)

### Analysis

**On first round (round=1):**
1. Identify the active code path using the linked binary's objdump. Read that specific block in the source.
2. Find the function's assembly in `libnn.objdump`:
   ```bash
   grep -A 200 '<function_name>:' ${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump | head -300
   ```
3. If the function calls sub-functions (GEMM kernels, etc.), also read those sub-functions' assembly and source
4. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md`, relevant `general/` pages for similar patterns, operator page if it exists
5. Compute key metrics from the assembly and source:
   - **Arithmetic Intensity** = FLOPs per byte transferred from memory. Count MAC-equivalent ops (e.g., each `vqmacc` contributes `2 * vl` FLOPs for LMUL-dependent `vl`); count bytes loaded/stored per loop iteration (load instructions x element width x vl). AI = FLOPs / bytes.
   - **Reuse Rate** = FLOPs per byte loaded (exclude stores). Measures how many times each loaded byte contributes to computation before being evicted. High reuse = cache-friendly; low reuse = memory-bound.
   - Save both as `arith_intensity` and `reuse_rate` in state.
6. Identify bottleneck using the metrics: low AI -> memory-bound (improve tiling/blocking/reuse); high AI but low IPC -> compute-bound (LMUL, register pressure, loop structure)
7. Form hypothesis: what to change and expected impact

**On subsequent rounds (round>1):**
1. Re-read the source (it changed last round)
2. Re-check the objdump for the updated assembly
3. Recompute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the updated assembly. Compare to previous round — did the change improve reuse or reduce memory traffic?
4. Review FPGA cycle/instruction delta from last round's `measure` step to identify new bottleneck
5. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant `general/` pages for the specific bottleneck pattern
6. Form the next hypothesis based on what the metrics reveal

**Proposer output:** 1–3 ranked ideas, each with what to change, which lines, expected impact rationale, and risk assessment. Include current arithmetic intensity and reuse rate in the summary. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.

### Output and Transitions

**Output:** Print the analysis summary (including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, and hypothesis). Do NOT wait for approval — proceed immediately.

**Decision gate:** If no concrete optimization opportunity can be identified (assembly looks clean, metrics are already optimal, LMUL/tiling are already well-tuned):
- Transition to idle with final summary.
- Do NOT force an optimization that has no clear rationale.

**Transition:** → `implement` (if opportunity found) or → `idle` (if no opportunity)

---

## State: `implement`

Apply the optimization.

1. Modify ONLY the active code path identified during `analyze`. The active path was determined by cross-referencing the linked binary's function calls with source `#ifdef` blocks. **Do NOT guess from `#ifdef` guards alone** — many macros (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO`) are defined in internal headers, not build.sh.
2. If the function calls sub-functions (e.g., a GEMM kernel), the optimization may need to be applied in the sub-function's source file, not the conv function's source.
3. Do NOT touch the plain C fallback or dead `#ifdef` blocks
4. Save a description of what was changed in state (`changes` field)
5. If this is round>1 and the previous round regressed, revert the regression first, then try the new approach

**CRITICAL — Intrinsic API selection:**
The active `ENA_VEC_ISA` code path uses **`NDS_VEC_*` macros** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`, `NDS_VEC_VNCLIP_WX`). These are defined in `internal/internal_vec_isa_v1_0.h`.

**NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics** (e.g., `__riscv_vle8_v_i8m2`, `__riscv_vqmacc_vx_i32m8`) in `ENA_VEC_ISA` blocks. Those require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is **NOT defined** for AX45MPV builds. Using them causes compilation failures.

When writing new RVV code, always:
- Study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef` block to learn the available `NDS_VEC_*` macro names
- Use explicit register names (`NDS_VEC_V0`, `NDS_VEC_V8`, `NDS_VEC_V16`, etc.)
- Match the LMUL register grouping: M8 uses v0/v8/v16/v24, M4 uses v0/v4/v8/.., M2 uses v0/v2/v4/..
- For loads: `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VLE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VLE32_V` (e32)
- For stores: `NDS_VEC_VSE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VSE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VSE32_V` (e32)

**Transition:** → `verify`

---

## State: `verify`

Incremental rebuild and QEMU correctness test.

1. Rebuild ONLY the modified source file (fast incremental):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh <source_file>
   ```
   Example:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
   ```

2. Compile the test binary (compile-only, no QEMU execution):
   ```bash
   TOOLCHAIN_BIN="/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
   export PATH="${TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${PATH}"
   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
   UNIT_TEST_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/Examples/unit_func"
   WORK_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/work"
   mkdir -p "${WORK_DIR}"
   riscv64-elf-gcc -O3 -Wall -Werror -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -Wl,-gc-sections \
     -mvh -mzfh -DENA_FS_VS \
     -mtune=andes-45-series -mext-vector=zve64d -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 \
     -DENA_FAST_ALGO -DENA_DUMP_GOLDEN=0 -DENA_MEASURE_PERF=0 \
     -DGETENV_TESTDIR=${UNIT_TEST_DIR} \
     -I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${UNIT_TEST_DIR}/include \
     -o "${WORK_DIR}/t_<test_name>.adx" \
     "${UNIT_TEST_DIR}/t_<test_name>.c" "${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.a" -lm
   ```

3. Run the compiled ELF via the **qemu-runner subagent** (handles semihosting fd 9 capture):
   ```
   Agent(subagent_type="qemu-runner", prompt="Run ELF: ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/work/t_<test_name>.adx at VLEN=1024 with flags: --cwd ${LIB_ROOT}/Examples/unit_func")
   ```
   Parse the output for "accuracy checking pass" / "accuracy checking fail".

4. Do NOT run full regression in the optimization loop — only run the target function's test. Full regression is too slow for iterative optimization.

**Pass:** → `measure`
**Compile error:** Launch a subagent to summarize the compilation error (what failed, which macro/function/type is missing, the exact error message). Then launch a NEW subagent with the summary to fix the code. Rebuild and re-verify. Max 3 retries before going back to `analyze` with a different approach.
**Test fail (compiled but wrong results):** → `implement` (analyze the failure, fix, retry. Max 3 retries before going back to `analyze` with a different approach)

---

## State: `measure`

Run on the AX45MPV FPGA board via GDB for ground-truth cycle counts.

```bash
/usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
```

This script:
1. Compiles the test with `-DPF_COUNTER -DENA_TEST_PERF -DENA_RUN_TWICE` flags
2. Uploads binary to FPGA via GDB expect script (`riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board`)
3. Runs on AX45MPV board (`sw-boards.andestech.com:1116`)
4. Prints cycle count: `"The cycle count is NNNN"`
5. Generates `.adx` and `.objdump` in `qemu-validate/work/`

Parse the output for:
- `"The cycle count is <N>"` — the FPGA cycle count
- `"The inst count is <N>"` — the FPGA instruction count
- `"accuracy checking pass/fail"` — correctness check

Save `cycles_after` and `instrs_after` in state. Compute speedup: `cycles_before / cycles_after`. Compute IPC: `instrs_after / cycles_after`.

**Transition:** → `learn`

---

## State: `learn`

Record the lesson, then loop back.

1. Update the wiki following the wiki-writer skill:
   - Read `.claude/skills/wiki-writer/SKILL.md`
   - Input context from state: function, source_file, round, hypothesis, changes, cycles_before, cycles_after, speedup, arith_intensity, reuse_rate, outcome
   - Execute all 4 operations: operator page, general page (if applicable), index, log
   - All content must follow the naive expression style defined in wiki-writer

2. Increment the `round` counter in state.

3. **Check exit condition:**
   - If `round >= 5`, STOP → `idle`. Print final summary of all rounds.
   - Otherwise, transition → `analyze` for the next optimization round on the same function.

4. When looping back to `analyze`, key inputs for the next round are: FPGA cycle/instruction counts from `measure`, updated arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from this round, updated assembly, and wiki pages. The `analyze` step will decide whether there's still an opportunity. If not, it exits to `idle`.

**Exit → `idle`:** Print a summary table of all rounds (round#, approach, cycles, instrs, IPC, AI, reuse_rate, speedup, insight). Instruction count diagnoses whether gains came from fewer instructions (algorithmic) or better IPC (cache/scheduling).
**Continue → `analyze`:** Print "Round N+1: continuing. AI=X, reuse_rate=Y, key finding: <insight>"

---

## State File Format

```json
{
  "step": "idle|select|analyze|implement|verify|measure|learn",
  "function": "function_name",
  "source_file": "Source/.../file.c",
  "test_name": "test_name_for_qemu",
  "round": 0,
  "hypothesis": "what we plan to change and why",
  "changes": "description of what was implemented",
  "cycles_before": "",
  "cycles_after": "",
  "instrs_before": "",
  "instrs_after": "",
  "speedup": "",
  "arith_intensity": "",
  "reuse_rate": "",
  "rounds_without_improvement": 0,
  "round_history": [
    {
      "round": 1,
      "approach": "...",
      "cycles": "...",
      "instrs": "...",
      "ipc": "...",
      "arith_intensity": "...",
      "reuse_rate": "...",
      "speedup": "...",
      "outcome": "improved|regressed|neutral",
      "insight": "..."
    }
  ],
  "history": ["timestamps of state transitions"]
}
```

Update state file on every transition:
```bash
python3 -c "
import json
from datetime import datetime
import subprocess
lib_root = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'rev-parse', '--show-toplevel']).decode().strip()
f = lib_root + '/.claude/opt-state.json'
state = json.load(open(f))
prev_step = state['step']
new_step = '<new_step>'
now = datetime.now()
state['step'] = new_step
state['step_started'] = now.isoformat()
state['history'].append(f'{now.isoformat()} {new_step}')
# Calculate time spent in previous step
if 'step_started' in state and state['history']:
    prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get('prev_step_started', now.isoformat()))
    elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
    state['last_step_duration'] = f'{prev_step}: {elapsed:.1f}s'
state['prev_step_started'] = state.get('step_started', now.isoformat())
json.dump(state, open(f, 'w'), indent=2)
print(json.dumps(state, indent=2))
"
```

---

## Subagent Dispatch

The main agent drives the FSM and reports progress to the user between every step. Only steps that require reasoning use subagents. Mechanical steps run commands directly.

| Step | Execution | Why |
|------|-----------|-----|
| **select** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just shell commands (grep, build, FPGA) |
| **analyze** | Subagent (proposer) | Requires reading code + assembly + metrics, forming hypothesis |
| **implement** | Subagent | Requires code editing with domain knowledge |
| **verify** | Main agent compiles test. qemu-runner subagent runs ELF. On compile error: subagent summarizes error → new subagent fixes code | Compile is mechanical; QEMU needs fd 9 handling via qemu-runner; error diagnosis and fix need reasoning |
| **measure** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just `run_fpga_test.sh` |
| **learn** | Main agent runs commands directly | Wiki page updates + state update |

### Subagent constraints

When dispatching to a subagent, always include these constraints in the prompt:

**For `analyze` subagent (proposer):**
- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (ONLY for `grep` on objdump)
- DO NOT edit any files
- DO NOT run build, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands
- **MUST use the linked test binary objdump** (`qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump`) to identify the active code path by checking which sub-functions are called (e.g., `jal` targets). Cross-reference with source `#ifdef` blocks. Never guess from source alone — `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` etc. are defined in internal headers.
- Focus on data flow, algorithm choice, tiling/blocking, loop structure, GEMM strategy, im2col, unrolling, LMUL selection, vector register utilization. Compute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the assembly. Must read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant wiki pages for what's been tried.
- Output: analysis summary including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, active code path (verified from objdump), and 1–3 ranked ideas with expected impact rationale, risk assessment, and which lines to modify

**For `verify error-summary` subagent (on compilation failure):**
- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob
- DO NOT edit any files or run any commands
- Input: the full compilation error output
- Output: concise summary — what failed, which macro/function/type is missing or misused, the exact error messages, and which source file/line caused it. This summary is passed to the fix subagent.

**For `verify error-fix` subagent (after error-summary):**
- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
- Input: the error summary from the error-summary subagent + the source file path
- Fix the compilation error based on the summary. Follow the same intrinsic rules as the implement subagent (NDS_VEC_* macros only, no __riscv_* intrinsics).
- Output: description of exact code change made to fix the error

**For `implement` subagent:**
- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
- DO NOT run build, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands
- DO NOT compile anything
- **CRITICAL: Use `NDS_VEC_*` macros ONLY** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`). NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics — they require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is NOT defined. Before writing new RVV code, read an existing working function in the same `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` block to learn the correct macro names.
- Output: description of exact code change made

---

## Rules

1. **Fully autonomous.** Do not wait for user input at any step. Run the entire loop.
2. **Main agent drives the FSM.** The main agent owns state, runs mechanical steps directly, dispatches reasoning steps to subagents, and reports progress to the user between every step.
3. **Never skip steps.** Follow FSM transitions strictly.
4. **Max 5 rounds per function.** After round 5, exit to idle regardless.
5. **analyze is the gatekeeper.** If it finds no optimization opportunity from code + assembly + metrics, exit to idle. Do not force pointless changes.
6. **FPGA is ground truth.** QEMU is correctness only.
7. **Always record lessons.** Wins AND losses. This compounds knowledge across functions.
8. **Target VLEN=1024.** Never sacrifice VLEN=1024 performance.
9. **If verify fails 3 times**, go back to `analyze` with a different approach, not the same one.
10. **If a round regresses**, revert the code before trying the next approach.
11. **Arithmetic intensity and reuse rate drive the next round.** Recompute them each round from updated assembly to track whether changes improved memory efficiency.
12. **Use `rebuild_single.sh` for incremental builds** after the initial `build_libnn.sh` in `select`. Never rebuild the full library in the optimization loop.
13. **Use `run_fpga_test.sh` for FPGA measurement.** It handles compilation with perf flags, GDB upload, and cycle count parsing. **NEVER compile or run FPGA tests manually — always use this script.**
14. **Only test the target function** during the optimization loop. Full regression is for final validation only.
15. **Scripts are the single source of truth for builds and tests.** Never construct gcc, QEMU, or GDB commands manually. Always use the scripts in `qemu-validate/`.

lastPrompt: read /optimize skill about the 'analyze' state. how to identify the active path?  Why do we identify from the assembly of objdump? We could compile the source code with '-E' to get the assembly code a…

content.text:
## Active path identification (current)

`analyze` greps the linked test binary's objdump for `jal`/`jalr` targets to see which sub-functions are actually called, then cross-references back to `#if/#elif` blocks in source (SKILL.md:88-94, 111-116).

## Why objdump instead of reading source

Because `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in **internal headers**, not just `build.sh` flags. `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally `#define`d in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`. Reading source + build.sh flags alone gives the wrong answer — you'd miss that `ENA_TILING` is on and edit the non-tiling branch (SKILL.md:108, 122-123).

## On `-E`

Terminology: `-E` emits preprocessed **C source**, not assembly (`-S` is assembly). The idea is sound though — preprocessed source directly shows which `#ifdef` branches survive, which is exactly the question.

Tradeoffs vs objdump:

| | `-E` preprocessed source | objdump of linked binary |
|---|---|---|
| Shows `#ifdef` resolution | Yes, directly | Indirectly (via call targets) |
| Shows inline code paths | Yes | No — only branches with external calls |
| Requires FPGA/link step | No | Yes (already produced in `select`) |
| Risk of flag drift | **High** — must replicate every `-D`, `-I`, `-m*` from build.sh exactly | **Zero** — reflects the actual build |
| Output size | Huge (all headers expanded) | Already scoped per function |

The flag-drift risk is the same failure mode we're trying to avoid: if you miss `-DENA_TILING` (or anything defined transitively), `-E` lies just like reading source lies. Objdump sidesteps that entirely because it's post-build.

**Recommendation:** keep objdump as the source of truth. If you want `-E` as a supplement (useful when the active block has no sub-function calls, so objdump can't disambiguate), drive it through `rebuild_single.sh` so flags are guaranteed to match — don't hand-construct the gcc line. A safe addition would be a `preprocess_single.sh` that reuses the exact CFLAGS from `rebuild_single.sh` but swaps in `-E`.

message.content: I don't understand the risk of flag drift. We use the same build script in two phase. one with '-E', the other without it. So we always use the same cmd as the built binary

content.text:
You're right - if both paths go through the same script with only `-E` swapped in, flag drift is not a risk. I overstated it.

So the real comparison becomes:

| | `-E` via shared script | objdump |
|---|---|---|
| Shows `#ifdef` resolution | Directly | Indirectly via call targets |
| Shows inline active blocks (no sub-calls) | Yes | No |
| Extra step needed | Run preprocess script | Already produced by `select` |

`-E` is actually the better primitive for the "which `#ifdef` branch is active" question - it answers it directly instead of inferring from call targets. Objdump's advantage (no extra step) is minor, and its weakness (can't disambiguate when the active block is fully inline with no external calls) is real.

Want me to add a `preprocess_single.sh` that mirrors `rebuild_single.sh`'s flags but uses `-E`, and update the `analyze` state to use it as the primary active-path check?

#!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# Incrementally rebuild a single source file and update libnn.a
# ~100x faster than full build.sh (1 file vs ~400 files)
#
# Usage:
#   rebuild_single.sh <source_file.c>
#
# Examples:
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c
#
# Prerequisites:
#   - libnn.a must already exist (run build_libnn.sh first for initial build)
#   - lib_objs/ directory must exist from prior build
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LIB_ROOT="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <source_file.c>" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SRC_FILE="$1"

# Resolve to absolute path if relative
if [[ "${SRC_FILE}" != /* ]]; then
    SRC_FILE="${LIB_ROOT}/${SRC_FILE}"
fi

if [ ! -f "${SRC_FILE}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Source file not found: ${SRC_FILE}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Paths
TOOLCHAIN_BIN="/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
export PATH="${TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${PATH}"

CC="riscv64-elf-gcc"
AR="riscv64-elf-ar"
RANLIB="riscv64-elf-ranlib"
OBJDUMP="riscv64-elf-objdump"

OBJ_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/lib_objs"
LIBNN="${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.a"

# Verify prerequisites
if [ ! -f "${LIBNN}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: libnn.a not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
    exit 1
fi
if [ ! -d "${OBJ_DIR}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: lib_objs/ not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# AX45MPV build flags (must match build.sh ax45mpv config)
CFLAGS="-I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${LIB_ROOT}/internal -c"
CFLAGS+=" -Wall -Werror -O3 -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections"
CFLAGS+=" -mtune=andes-45-series -fno-strict-aliasing"
CFLAGS+=" -mext-vector -DENA_VEC_ISA -DMAX_VLEN=1024"
CFLAGS+=" -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 -DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"
CFLAGS+=" -fno-tree-slp-vectorize -fno-tree-vectorize"

# Detect zfh support
if ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null &> /dev/null; then
    ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null 2>/dev/null | grep -q '__riscv_float_abi_single\|__riscv_float_abi_double' && CFLAGS+=" -mzfh"
fi

# Object file name (strip path, change .c to .o)
OBJ_NAME="$(basename "${SRC_FILE}" .c).o"
OBJ_FILE="${OBJ_DIR}/${OBJ_NAME}"

# Step 1: Recompile single file
echo "[rebuild] ${SRC_FILE##*/}"
${CC} ${CFLAGS} -o "${OBJ_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"

# Step 2: Replace object in archive
echo "[archive] updating ${OBJ_NAME} in libnn.a"
${AR} rD "${LIBNN}" "${OBJ_FILE}"
${RANLIB} -D "${LIBNN}"

# Step 3: Regenerate objdump
echo "[objdump] libnn.objdump"
${OBJDUMP} -S "${LIBNN}" > "${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump"

echo "[done] Rebuilt ${OBJ_NAME} in $(date +%Ts)"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# Preprocess a single source file with -E using the SAME flags as
# rebuild_single.sh. Use this to identify the active #ifdef path in analyze:
# the output shows exactly which branches survive preprocessing.
#
# Usage:
#   preprocess_single.sh <source_file.c> [output_file]
#
# If output_file is omitted, writes to qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LIB_ROOT="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <source_file.c> [output_file]" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SRC_FILE="$1"
if [[ "${SRC_FILE}" != /* ]]; then
    SRC_FILE="${LIB_ROOT}/${SRC_FILE}"
fi

if [ ! -f "${SRC_FILE}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Source file not found: ${SRC_FILE}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

WORK_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/work"
mkdir -p "${WORK_DIR}"

OUT_FILE="${2:-${WORK_DIR}/$(basename "${SRC_FILE}" .c).i}"

TOOLCHAIN_BIN="/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
export PATH="${TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${PATH}"

CC="riscv64-elf-gcc"

# MUST match rebuild_single.sh exactly (minus -c, plus -E -P)
# -P suppresses line markers so the output is cleaner to grep
CFLAGS="-I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${LIB_ROOT}/internal"
CFLAGS+=" -Wall -Werror -O3 -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections"
CFLAGS+=" -mtune=andes-45-series -fno-strict-aliasing"
CFLAGS+=" -mext-vector -DENA_VEC_ISA -DMAX_VLEN=1024"
CFLAGS+=" -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 -DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"
CFLAGS+=" -fno-tree-slp-vectorize -fno-tree-vectorize"

if ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null &> /dev/null; then
    ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null 2>/dev/null | grep -q '__riscv_float_abi_single\|__riscv_float_abi_double' && CFLAGS+=" -mzfh"
fi

echo "[preprocess] ${SRC_FILE##*/} -> ${OUT_FILE##*/}"
${CC} ${CFLAGS} -E -P -o "${OUT_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"
echo "[done] ${OUT_FILE}"

**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from the COMPILED BINARY, not from source `#ifdef` guards.**

Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from source alone.** Instead:

1. Check the linked test binary's objdump for what functions are actually called:
   ```bash
   # Look at the perf objdump from the select step's FPGA run
   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
   ```
2. Cross-reference the called sub-functions (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) back to the source to identify which `#if/#elif` block is active.
3. Read only the ACTIVE block. Modifying dead code has zero effect.
input.new_string:
**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from preprocessed source, not from raw `#ifdef` guards.**

Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from raw source alone.** Instead:

1. **Primary method — preprocessed source.** Run the file through the preprocessor with the exact build flags:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/preprocess_single.sh <source_file.c>
   # Output: qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i
   ```
   The `.i` file contains only the branches that survive preprocessing — it directly answers "which `#ifdef` block is active" without inference. Grep for the function in the `.i` file and read that body. This works even when the active block is fully inline (no sub-function calls), which objdump cannot disambiguate.

2. **Secondary method — objdump cross-check.** For functions that dispatch to sub-kernels, confirm by checking which sub-functions the linked binary actually calls:
   ```bash
   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
   ```
   Cross-reference call targets (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) with the preprocessed source. This catches cases where LTO/dead-code elimination pruned a branch that `-E` still shows.

3. Read only the ACTIVE block. Modifying dead code has zero effect.

Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from raw source alone.** Instead:

1. **Primary method — preprocessed source.** Run the file through the preprocessor with the exact build flags:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/preprocess_single.sh <source_file.c>
   # Output: qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i
   ```
   The `.i` file contains only the branches that survive preprocessing — it directly answers "which `#ifdef` block is active" without inference. Grep for the function in the `.i` file and read that body. This works even when the active block is fully inline (no sub-function calls), which objdump cannot disambiguate.

2. **Secondary method — objdump cross-check.** For functions that dispatch to sub-kernels, confirm by checking which sub-functions the linked binary actually calls:
   ```bash
   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
   ```
   Cross-reference call targets (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) with the preprocessed source. This catches cases where LTO/dead-code elimination pruned a branch that `-E` still shows.

3. Read only the ACTIVE block. Modifying dead code has zero effect.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Usage:
#   rebuild_single.sh <source_file.c>
#
# Examples:
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c
#
# Prerequisites:
#   - libnn.a must already exist (run build_libnn.sh first for initial build)
#   - lib_objs/ directory must exist from prior build
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LIB_ROOT="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <source_file.c>" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SRC_FILE="$1"
input.new_string:
# Usage:
#   rebuild_single.sh <source_file.c>           # compile + update libnn.a + objdump
#   rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>        # preprocess only; write .i to qemu-validate/work/
#
# The -E mode reuses the EXACT same flags as the compile mode. Use it in the
# analyze step to see which #ifdef branches survive preprocessing (i.e. the
# active code path) without any flag drift.
#
# Examples:
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
#   rebuild_single.sh -E Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c
#
# Prerequisites:
#   - libnn.a must already exist (run build_libnn.sh first for initial build)
#   - lib_objs/ directory must exist from prior build
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LIB_ROOT="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

PREPROCESS_ONLY=0
if [ "${1:-}" = "-E" ]; then
    PREPROCESS_ONLY=1
    shift
fi

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 [-E] <source_file.c>" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 -E Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c  (preprocess only)" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SRC_FILE="$1"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# Incrementally rebuild a single source file and update libnn.a
# ~100x faster than full build.sh (1 file vs ~400 files)
#
# Usage:
#   rebuild_single.sh <source_file.c>
#
# Examples:
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c
#
# Prerequisites:
#   - libnn.a must already exist (run build_libnn.sh first for initial build)
#   - lib_objs/ directory must exist from prior build
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LIB_ROOT="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <source_file.c>" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SRC_FILE="$1"

# Resolve to absolute path if relative
if [[ "${SRC_FILE}" != /* ]]; then
    SRC_FILE="${LIB_ROOT}/${SRC_FILE}"
fi

if [ ! -f "${SRC_FILE}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Source file not found: ${SRC_FILE}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Paths
TOOLCHAIN_BIN="/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
export PATH="${TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${PATH}"

CC="riscv64-elf-gcc"
AR="riscv64-elf-ar"
RANLIB="riscv64-elf-ranlib"
OBJDUMP="riscv64-elf-objdump"

OBJ_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/lib_objs"
LIBNN="${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.a"

# Verify prerequisites
if [ ! -f "${LIBNN}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: libnn.a not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
    exit 1
fi
if [ ! -d "${OBJ_DIR}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: lib_objs/ not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# AX45MPV build flags (must match build.sh ax45mpv config)
CFLAGS="-I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${LIB_ROOT}/internal -c"
CFLAGS+=" -Wall -Werror -O3 -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections"
CFLAGS+=" -mtune=andes-45-series -fno-strict-aliasing"
CFLAGS+=" -mext-vector -DENA_VEC_ISA -DMAX_VLEN=1024"
CFLAGS+=" -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 -DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"
CFLAGS+=" -fno-tree-slp-vectorize -fno-tree-vectorize"

# Detect zfh support
if ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null &> /dev/null; then
    ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null 2>/dev/null | grep -q '__riscv_float_abi_single\|__riscv_float_abi_double' && CFLAGS+=" -mzfh"
fi

# Object file name (strip path, change .c to .o)
OBJ_NAME="$(basename "${SRC_FILE}" .c).o"
OBJ_FILE="${OBJ_DIR}/${OBJ_NAME}"

# Step 1: Recompile single file
echo "[rebuild] ${SRC_FILE##*/}"
${CC} ${CFLAGS} -o "${OBJ_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"

# Step 2: Replace object in archive
echo "[archive] updating ${OBJ_NAME} in libnn.a"
${AR} rD "${LIBNN}" "${OBJ_FILE}"
${RANLIB} -D "${LIBNN}"

# Step 3: Regenerate objdump
echo "[objdump] libnn.objdump"
${OBJDUMP} -S "${LIBNN}" > "${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump"

echo "[done] Rebuilt ${OBJ_NAME} in $(date +%Ts)"

lines: # ~100x faster than full build.sh (1 file vs ~400 files)
+#   rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>        # preprocess only; write .i to qemu-validate/work/
+# The -E mode reuses the EXACT same flags as the compile mode. Use it in the
+# analyze step to see which #ifdef branches survive preprocessing (i.e. the
+# active code path) without any flag drift.
#   - libnn.a must already exist (run build_libnn.sh first for initial build)

    exit 1
fi
if [ ! -d "${OBJ_DIR}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: lib_objs/ not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# AX45MPV build flags (must match build.sh ax45mpv config)
CFLAGS="-I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${LIB_ROOT}/internal -c"
input.new_string:
# Verify prerequisites (compile mode only; preprocess mode doesn't need libnn.a)
if [ ${PREPROCESS_ONLY} -eq 0 ]; then
    if [ ! -f "${LIBNN}" ]; then
        echo "ERROR: libnn.a not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    if [ ! -d "${OBJ_DIR}" ]; then
        echo "ERROR: lib_objs/ not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# AX45MPV build flags (must match build.sh ax45mpv config)
CFLAGS="-I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${LIB_ROOT}/internal -c"
