attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Prerequisites only matter for compile mode; -E mode just needs the toolchain.
if [ ${PREPROCESS_ONLY} -eq 0 ]; then

    [ -d "${OBJ_DIR}" ] || { echo "ERROR: lib_objs/ not found at ${OBJ_DIR}. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2; exit 1; }

${CFG_CC} ${CFLAGS} -o "${OBJ_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"

echo "[archive] updating ${OBJ_NAME} in libnn.a"
${CFG_AR} rD "${LIBNN}" "${OBJ_FILE}"
${CFG_RANLIB} -D "${LIBNN}"

echo "[objdump] libnn.objdump"
${CFG_OBJDUMP} -S "${LIBNN}" > "${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.objdump"

echo "[done] Rebuilt ${OBJ_NAME}"

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# Incrementally rebuild a single source file and update libnn.a
# ~100x faster than full build.sh (1 file vs ~400 files)
#
# Usage:
#   rebuild_single.sh <source_file.c>           # compile + update libnn.a + objdump
#   rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>        # preprocess only; write .i to qemu-validate/work/
#
# The -E mode reuses the EXACT same flags as the compile mode. Use it in the
# analyze step to see which #ifdef branches survive preprocessing (i.e. the
# active code path) without any flag drift.
#
# Examples:
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
#   rebuild_single.sh -E Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c
#
# Prerequisites:
#   - libnn.a must already exist (run build_libnn.sh first for initial build)
#   - lib_objs/ directory must exist from prior build
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LIB_ROOT="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

PREPROCESS_ONLY=0
if [ "${1:-}" = "-E" ]; then
    PREPROCESS_ONLY=1
    shift
fi

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 [-E] <source_file.c>" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 -E Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c  (preprocess only)" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SRC_FILE="$1"

# Resolve to absolute path if relative
if [[ "${SRC_FILE}" != /* ]]; then
    SRC_FILE="${LIB_ROOT}/${SRC_FILE}"
fi

if [ ! -f "${SRC_FILE}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Source file not found: ${SRC_FILE}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Paths
TOOLCHAIN_BIN="/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
export PATH="${TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${PATH}"

CC="riscv64-elf-gcc"
AR="riscv64-elf-ar"
RANLIB="riscv64-elf-ranlib"
OBJDUMP="riscv64-elf-objdump"

OBJ_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/lib_objs"
LIBNN="${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.a"

# Verify prerequisites (compile mode only; preprocess mode doesn't need libnn.a)
if [ ${PREPROCESS_ONLY} -eq 0 ]; then
    if [ ! -f "${LIBNN}" ]; then
        echo "ERROR: libnn.a not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    if [ ! -d "${OBJ_DIR}" ]; then
        echo "ERROR: lib_objs/ not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# AX45MPV build flags (must match build.sh ax45mpv config)
CFLAGS="-I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${LIB_ROOT}/internal -c"
CFLAGS+=" -Wall -Werror -O3 -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections"
CFLAGS+=" -mtune=andes-45-series -fno-strict-aliasing"
CFLAGS+=" -mext-vector -DENA_VEC_ISA -DMAX_VLEN=1024"
CFLAGS+=" -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 -DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"
CFLAGS+=" -fno-tree-slp-vectorize -fno-tree-vectorize"

# Detect zfh support
if ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null &> /dev/null; then
    ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null 2>/dev/null | grep -q '__riscv_float_abi_single\|__riscv_float_abi_double' && CFLAGS+=" -mzfh"
fi

# Preprocess-only path: reuse the exact same CFLAGS, swap -c for -E -P.
if [ ${PREPROCESS_ONLY} -eq 1 ]; then
    WORK_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/work"
    mkdir -p "${WORK_DIR}"
    OUT_FILE="${WORK_DIR}/$(basename "${SRC_FILE}" .c).i"
    # Strip -c from CFLAGS and add -E -P (no line markers for easier grepping)
    PP_FLAGS="${CFLAGS/-c/-E -P}"
    echo "[preprocess] ${SRC_FILE##*/} -> ${OUT_FILE##*/}"
    ${CC} ${PP_FLAGS} -o "${OUT_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"
    echo "[done] ${OUT_FILE}"
    exit 0
fi

# Object file name (strip path, change .c to .o)
OBJ_NAME="$(basename "${SRC_FILE}" .c).o"
OBJ_FILE="${OBJ_DIR}/${OBJ_NAME}"

# Step 1: Recompile single file
echo "[rebuild] ${SRC_FILE##*/}"
${CC} ${CFLAGS} -o "${OBJ_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"

# Step 2: Replace object in archive
echo "[archive] updating ${OBJ_NAME} in libnn.a"
${AR} rD "${LIBNN}" "${OBJ_FILE}"
${RANLIB} -D "${LIBNN}"

# Step 3: Regenerate objdump
echo "[objdump] libnn.objdump"
${OBJDUMP} -S "${LIBNN}" > "${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump"

echo "[done] Rebuilt ${OBJ_NAME} in $(date +%Ts)"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# Incrementally rebuild a single source file and update libnn.a
# ~100x faster than full build.sh (1 file vs ~400 files)
#
# Usage:
#   rebuild_single.sh <source_file.c>           # compile + update libnn.a + objdump
#   rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>        # preprocess only; write .i to qemu-validate/work/
#
# The -E mode reuses the EXACT same flags as the compile mode. Use it in the
# analyze step to see which #ifdef branches survive preprocessing (i.e. the
# active code path) without any flag drift.
#
# Examples:
#   rebuild_single.sh Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c
#   rebuild_single.sh -E Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c
#
# Prerequisites:
#   - libnn.a must already exist (run build_libnn.sh first for initial build)
#   - lib_objs/ directory must exist from prior build
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LIB_ROOT="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

PREPROCESS_ONLY=0
if [ "${1:-}" = "-E" ]; then
    PREPROCESS_ONLY=1
    shift
fi

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 [-E] <source_file.c>" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_any.c" >&2
    echo "  e.g.: $0 -E Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_maxpool_HWC_s8.c  (preprocess only)" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SRC_FILE="$1"

# Resolve to absolute path if relative
if [[ "${SRC_FILE}" != /* ]]; then
    SRC_FILE="${LIB_ROOT}/${SRC_FILE}"
fi

if [ ! -f "${SRC_FILE}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Source file not found: ${SRC_FILE}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Paths
TOOLCHAIN_BIN="/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
export PATH="${TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${PATH}"

CC="riscv64-elf-gcc"
AR="riscv64-elf-ar"
RANLIB="riscv64-elf-ranlib"
OBJDUMP="riscv64-elf-objdump"

OBJ_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/lib_objs"
LIBNN="${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.a"

# Verify prerequisites (compile mode only; preprocess mode doesn't need libnn.a)
if [ ${PREPROCESS_ONLY} -eq 0 ]; then
    if [ ! -f "${LIBNN}" ]; then
        echo "ERROR: libnn.a not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    if [ ! -d "${OBJ_DIR}" ]; then
        echo "ERROR: lib_objs/ not found. Run build_libnn.sh first." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# AX45MPV build flags (must match build.sh ax45mpv config)
CFLAGS="-I${LIB_ROOT}/Include -I${LIB_ROOT}/internal -c"
CFLAGS+=" -Wall -Werror -O3 -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections"
CFLAGS+=" -mtune=andes-45-series -fno-strict-aliasing"
CFLAGS+=" -mext-vector -DENA_VEC_ISA -DMAX_VLEN=1024"
CFLAGS+=" -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 -DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"
CFLAGS+=" -fno-tree-slp-vectorize -fno-tree-vectorize"

# Detect zfh support
if ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null &> /dev/null; then
    ${CC} -mzfh -E -dM ${CFLAGS} - < /dev/null 2>/dev/null | grep -q '__riscv_float_abi_single\|__riscv_float_abi_double' && CFLAGS+=" -mzfh"
fi

# Preprocess-only path: reuse the exact same CFLAGS, swap -c for -E -P.
if [ ${PREPROCESS_ONLY} -eq 1 ]; then
    WORK_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/work"
    mkdir -p "${WORK_DIR}"
    OUT_FILE="${WORK_DIR}/$(basename "${SRC_FILE}" .c).i"
    # Strip -c from CFLAGS and add -E -P (no line markers for easier grepping)
    PP_FLAGS="${CFLAGS/-c/-E -P}"
    echo "[preprocess] ${SRC_FILE##*/} -> ${OUT_FILE##*/}"
    ${CC} ${PP_FLAGS} -o "${OUT_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"
    echo "[done] ${OUT_FILE}"
    exit 0
fi

# Object file name (strip path, change .c to .o)
OBJ_NAME="$(basename "${SRC_FILE}" .c).o"
OBJ_FILE="${OBJ_DIR}/${OBJ_NAME}"

# Step 1: Recompile single file
echo "[rebuild] ${SRC_FILE##*/}"
${CC} ${CFLAGS} -o "${OBJ_FILE}" "${SRC_FILE}"

# Step 2: Replace object in archive
echo "[archive] updating ${OBJ_NAME} in libnn.a"
${AR} rD "${LIBNN}" "${OBJ_FILE}"
${RANLIB} -D "${LIBNN}"

# Step 3: Regenerate objdump
echo "[objdump] libnn.objdump"
${OBJDUMP} -S "${LIBNN}" > "${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump"

echo "[done] Rebuilt ${OBJ_NAME} in $(date +%Ts)"

content.content:
1	---
2	name: optimize
3	description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
4	argument-hint: [function_name|status|reset]
5	disable-model-invocation: false
6	allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
7	---
8	
9	# libnn RVV Optimization Skill
10	
11	Fully autonomous optimization loop. State persisted in `.claude/opt-state.json`.
12	
13	## Current State
14	
15	!`LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel); cat ${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/opt-state.json 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`
16	
17	## Commands
18	
19	- `/optimize <function_name>` — Start optimizing a function (loops until flat land).
20	- `/optimize status` — Show current state
21	- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle
22	
23	Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`
24	
25	## Finite State Machine
26	
27	```
28	idle → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
29	                  ↑  |         ↑          |  |                          |
30	                  |  |         └──────────┘  |                          |
31	                  |  |      (test fail → fix)|                          |
32	                  |  |                       └→ analyze                 |
33	                  |  |                (3 retries exhausted)             |
34	                  |  └→ idle (no opportunity found)                     |
35	                  |                                                     |
36	                  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
37	                    (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)
38	
39	Exit conditions:
40	  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
41	  - max 5 rounds per function → idle (see Rules #4)
42	```
43	
44	The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from FPGA measurements, updated assembly, and recomputed metrics. The verify/measure loop provides real feedback.
45	
46	---
47	
48	## Build Scripts Reference
49	
50	All scripts are at `qemu-validate/` (relative to project root). Always use `/usr/bin/bash` to invoke them. In every bash block, define: `LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)`
51	
52	| Script | Purpose | Time |
53	|--------|---------|------|
54	| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files). Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
55	| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
56	| `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` | Preprocess only (reuses the same flags). Output: `qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i`. Use in `analyze` to see which `#ifdef` branches survive. | ~0.3s |
57	| `run_qemu_test.sh <name> --strace --vlen 1024` | Build test binary + run on QEMU with fd-9 strace capture + parse pass/fail. Use in `verify`. | ~2s |
58	| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. | ~30s |
59	
60	---
61	
62	## State: `idle`
63	
64	No optimization in progress.
65	
66	If a function name is provided, transition → `select`.
67	If "status", report idle. If "reset", clear state.
68	
69	---
70	
71	## State: `select`
72	
73	Validate the function and gather baseline context.
74	
75	1. Find the source file under `Source/` — grep for the function name
76	2. Confirm it has `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` (existing RVV to improve)
77	3. Find corresponding test in `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`
78	4. Query the wiki for prior lessons and applicable techniques:
79	   - Read `docs/wiki/index.md` to identify relevant pages
80	   - Read the operator page in `docs/wiki/operators/` if it exists (e.g., `conv-hwc-s8-sym-bias-fast.md`)
81	   - Read matching `docs/wiki/general/` pages for applicable techniques and known anti-patterns
82	5. Full build (first time only):
83	   ```bash
84	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/build_libnn.sh
85	   ```
86	6. Run baseline FPGA measurement (**MUST use this exact script — do NOT compile or run FPGA tests manually**):
87	   ```bash
88	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
89	   ```
90	   Parse both counts from the output lines (same format as `measure` state):
91	   - `"The cycle count is <N>"` → `cycles_before`
92	   - `"The inst count is <N>"` → `instrs_before`
93	   (The `RESULT: PASS (cycles=N)` summary line also carries cycles but not instrs, so use the raw lines for both.)
94	7. **Identify the active code path.** Primary: preprocess the source with the same build flags and read the surviving `#ifdef` branches:
95	   ```bash
96	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>
97	   # Output: qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i
98	   ```
99	   Secondary (for dispatch functions that call sub-kernels): confirm the linked sub-function names from the perf objdump:
100	   ```bash
101	   grep -A500 '<function_name>:' qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | \
102	     grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" | head -20
103	   ```
104	   Save `active_path_file` (path to `.i`) and `called_functions` in state.
105	8. Save state: function, source_file, test_name, cycles_before, instrs_before, active_path_file, called_functions.
106	
107	**Transition:** → `analyze`
108	**Fail:** Function not found or no RVV → stay `select`, report error.
109	
110	---
111	
112	## State: `analyze`
113	
114	Study the current RVV code and assembly to form the next optimization hypothesis.
115	
116	**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from preprocessed source, not from raw `#ifdef` guards.**
117	
118	Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from raw source alone.** Instead:
119	
120	1. **Primary method — preprocessed source.** Run the file through the preprocessor with the exact build flags:
121	   ```bash
122	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>
123	   # Output: qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i
124	   ```
125	   The `.i` file contains only the branches that survive preprocessing — it directly answers "which `#ifdef` block is active" without inference. Grep for the function in the `.i` file and read that body. This works even when the active block is fully inline (no sub-function calls), which objdump cannot disambiguate.
126	
127	2. **Secondary method — objdump cross-check.** For functions that dispatch to sub-kernels, confirm by checking which sub-functions the linked binary actually calls:
128	   ```bash
129	   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
130	   ```
131	   Cross-reference call targets (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) with the preprocessed source. This catches cases where LTO/dead-code elimination pruned a branch that `-E` still shows.
132	
133	3. When mapping the `.i` body back to raw source for editing, target only the `#ifdef` branch whose body matches the `.i`. Modifying any other branch has zero effect.
134	
135	**Quick reference of known-active defines for AX45MPV** (the `.i` file is authoritative — treat this list as a hint, not ground truth):
136	- `ENA_VEC_ISA`, `NDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000`, `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD` — from build.sh
137	- `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` — unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`
138	- `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC`, `ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC` — typically NOT defined (confirm against `.i`)
139	
140	### Analysis
141	
142	**On first round (round=1):**
143	1. Identify the active code path from the `.i` file saved in `active_path_file` by `select`. Read the function body there to understand what will actually execute.
144	2. Find the function's assembly in `libnn.objdump`:
145	   ```bash
146	   grep -A 200 '<function_name>:' ${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump | head -300
147	   ```
148	3. If the function calls sub-functions (GEMM kernels, etc.), run `rebuild_single.sh -E` on each sub-function's source file too, and read its assembly from `libnn.objdump`. Sub-functions have their own `#ifdef` branches.
149	4. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md`, relevant `general/` pages for similar patterns, operator page if it exists
150	5. Compute key metrics from the assembly and source:
151	   - **Arithmetic Intensity** = FLOPs per byte transferred from memory. Count MAC-equivalent ops (e.g., each `vqmacc` contributes `2 * vl` FLOPs for LMUL-dependent `vl`); count bytes loaded/stored per loop iteration (load instructions x element width x vl). AI = FLOPs / bytes.
152	   - **Reuse Rate** = FLOPs per byte loaded (exclude stores). Measures how many times each loaded byte contributes to computation before being evicted. High reuse = cache-friendly; low reuse = memory-bound.
153	   - Save both as `arith_intensity` and `reuse_rate` in state.
154	6. Identify bottleneck using the metrics: low AI -> memory-bound (improve tiling/blocking/reuse); high AI but low IPC -> compute-bound (LMUL, register pressure, loop structure)
155	7. Form hypothesis: what to change and expected impact
156	
157	**On subsequent rounds (round>1):**
158	1. Re-run `rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>` to refresh the `.i` (the previous round's `implement` modified the source, and if any macros were touched the `#ifdef` resolution may have shifted). Re-read the active block.
159	2. Re-check `libnn.objdump` for the updated assembly of the function.
160	3. Recompute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the updated assembly. Compare to previous round — did the change improve reuse or reduce memory traffic?
161	4. Review FPGA cycle/instruction delta from last round's `measure` step to identify new bottleneck
162	5. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant `general/` pages for the specific bottleneck pattern
163	6. Form the next hypothesis based on what the metrics reveal
164	
165	**Proposer output:** 1–3 ranked ideas, each with what to change, which lines, expected impact rationale, and risk assessment. Include current arithmetic intensity and reuse rate in the summary. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.
166	
167	### Output and Transitions
168	
169	**Output:** Print the analysis summary (including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, and hypothesis). Do NOT wait for approval — proceed immediately.
170	
171	**Decision gate:** If no concrete optimization opportunity can be identified (assembly looks clean, metrics are already optimal, LMUL/tiling are already well-tuned):
172	- Transition to idle with final summary.
173	- Do NOT force an optimization that has no clear rationale.
174	
175	**Transition:** → `implement` (if opportunity found) or → `idle` (if no opportunity)
176	
177	---
178	
179	## State: `implement`
180	
181	Apply the optimization.
182	
183	1. Modify ONLY the active code path identified during `analyze`. The active path was determined from the preprocessed `.i` file (with objdump cross-check for dispatch functions). **Do NOT guess from `#ifdef` guards in the raw source** — many macros (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO`) are defined in internal headers, not build.sh. Map from the `.i` body back to the corresponding raw-source line numbers when editing.
184	2. If the function calls sub-functions (e.g., a GEMM kernel), the optimization may need to be applied in the sub-function's source file, not the conv function's source.
185	3. Do NOT touch the plain C fallback or dead `#ifdef` blocks
186	4. Save a description of what was changed in state (`changes` field)
187	5. If this is round>1 and the previous round regressed, revert the regression first, then try the new approach
188	
189	**CRITICAL — Intrinsic API selection:**
190	The active `ENA_VEC_ISA` code path uses **`NDS_VEC_*` macros** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`, `NDS_VEC_VNCLIP_WX`). These are defined in `internal/internal_vec_isa_v1_0.h`.
191	
192	**NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics** (e.g., `__riscv_vle8_v_i8m2`, `__riscv_vqmacc_vx_i32m8`) in `ENA_VEC_ISA` blocks. Those require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is **NOT defined** for AX45MPV builds. Using them causes compilation failures.
193	
194	When writing new RVV code, always:
195	- Study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef` block to learn the available `NDS_VEC_*` macro names
196	- Use explicit register names (`NDS_VEC_V0`, `NDS_VEC_V8`, `NDS_VEC_V16`, etc.)
197	- Match the LMUL register grouping: M8 uses v0/v8/v16/v24, M4 uses v0/v4/v8/.., M2 uses v0/v2/v4/..
198	- For loads: `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VLE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VLE32_V` (e32)
199	- For stores: `NDS_VEC_VSE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VSE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VSE32_V` (e32)
200	
201	**Transition:** → `verify`
202	
203	---
204	
205	## State: `verify`
206	
207	Incremental rebuild and QEMU correctness test — all via scripts.
208	
209	1. Rebuild ONLY the modified libnn source (fast incremental):
210	   ```bash
211	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh <source_file>
212	   ```
213	
214	2. Build the test binary AND run it on QEMU with fd-9 semihosting capture in one script call:
215	   ```bash
216	   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh <test_name> --strace --vlen 1024
217	   ```
218	   The script compiles `Examples/unit_func/t_<test_name>.c` against `libnn.a` with the canonical CFLAGS, runs under `strace -e trace=write` to capture fd-9 output, and emits one of:
219	   - `RESULT: PASS (N sub-tests passed)` — correctness verified
220	   - `RESULT: FAIL (N failures detected)` — compiled but wrong results
221	   - `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR` — gcc failed; full error is on stderr
222	   - `RESULT: TIMEOUT (Ns)` — QEMU hang
223	   - `RESULT: UNKNOWN (...)` — no pass/fail marker in output
224	
225	3. Do NOT run full regression in the optimization loop — only the target function's test.
226	
227	**Pass:** → `measure`
228	**`COMPILE_ERROR`:** Launch ONE `error-fix` subagent with the full stderr + source file path. It reads the error and edits the source. Re-run step 1+2. Max 3 compile-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
229	**`FAIL` (wrong results):** → `implement` (diagnose + fix). Max 3 test-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
230	**`TIMEOUT` / `UNKNOWN`:** Treat as FAIL, same retry policy.
231	
232	---
233	
234	## State: `measure`
235	
236	Run on the AX45MPV FPGA board via GDB for ground-truth cycle counts.
237	
238	```bash
239	/usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
240	```
241	
242	This script:
243	1. Compiles the test with `-DPF_COUNTER -DENA_TEST_PERF -DENA_RUN_TWICE` flags
244	2. Uploads binary to FPGA via GDB expect script (`riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board`)
245	3. Runs on AX45MPV board (`sw-boards.andestech.com:1116`)
246	4. Prints cycle count: `"The cycle count is NNNN"`
247	5. Generates `.adx` and `.objdump` in `qemu-validate/work/`
248	
249	Parse the output for:
250	- `"The cycle count is <N>"` — the FPGA cycle count
251	- `"The inst count is <N>"` — the FPGA instruction count
252	- `"accuracy checking pass/fail"` — correctness check
253	
254	Save `cycles_after` and `instrs_after` in state. Compute speedup: `cycles_before / cycles_after`. Compute IPC: `instrs_after / cycles_after`.
255	
256	**Transition:** → `learn`
257	
258	---
259	
260	## State: `learn`
261	
262	Record the lesson, then loop back.
263	
264	1. Update the wiki following the wiki-writer skill:
265	   - Read `.claude/skills/wiki-writer/SKILL.md`
266	   - Input context from state: function, source_file, round, hypothesis, changes, cycles_before, cycles_after, speedup, arith_intensity, reuse_rate, outcome
267	   - Execute all 4 operations: operator page, general page (if applicable), index, log
268	   - All content must follow the naive expression style defined in wiki-writer
269	
270	2. Increment the `round` counter in state.
271	
272	3. **Check exit condition:**
273	   - If `round >= 5`, STOP → `idle`. Print final summary of all rounds.
274	   - Otherwise, transition → `analyze` for the next optimization round on the same function.
275	
276	4. When looping back to `analyze`, key inputs for the next round are: FPGA cycle/instruction counts from `measure`, updated arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from this round, updated assembly, and wiki pages. The `analyze` step will decide whether there's still an opportunity. If not, it exits to `idle`.
277	
278	**Exit → `idle`:** Print a summary table of all rounds (round#, approach, cycles, instrs, IPC, AI, reuse_rate, speedup, insight). Instruction count diagnoses whether gains came from fewer instructions (algorithmic) or better IPC (cache/scheduling).
279	**Continue → `analyze`:** Print "Round N+1: continuing. AI=X, reuse_rate=Y, key finding: <insight>"
280	
281	---
282	
283	## State File Format
284	
285	```json
286	{
287	  "step": "idle|select|analyze|implement|verify|measure|learn",
288	  "function": "function_name",
289	  "source_file": "Source/.../file.c",
290	  "test_name": "test_name_for_qemu",
291	  "round": 0,
292	  "hypothesis": "what we plan to change and why",
293	  "changes": "description of what was implemented",
294	  "cycles_before": "",
295	  "cycles_after": "",
296	  "instrs_before": "",
297	  "instrs_after": "",
298	  "speedup": "",
299	  "arith_intensity": "",
300	  "reuse_rate": "",
301	  "active_path_file": "",
302	  "called_functions": [],
303	  "round_history": [
304	    {
305	      "round": 1,
306	      "approach": "...",
307	      "cycles": "...",
308	      "instrs": "...",
309	      "ipc": "...",
310	      "arith_intensity": "...",
311	      "reuse_rate": "...",
312	      "speedup": "...",
313	      "outcome": "improved|regressed|neutral",
314	      "insight": "..."
315	    }
316	  ],
317	  "history": ["timestamps of state transitions"]
318	}
319	```
320	
321	Update state file on every transition:
322	```bash
323	python3 -c "
324	import json
325	from datetime import datetime
326	import subprocess
327	lib_root = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'rev-parse', '--show-toplevel']).decode().strip()
328	f = lib_root + '/.claude/opt-state.json'
329	state = json.load(open(f))
330	prev_step = state['step']
331	new_step = '<new_step>'
332	now = datetime.now()
333	state['step'] = new_step
334	state['step_started'] = now.isoformat()
335	state['history'].append(f'{now.isoformat()} {new_step}')
336	# Calculate time spent in previous step
337	if 'step_started' in state and state['history']:
338	    prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get('prev_step_started', now.isoformat()))
339	    elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
340	    state['last_step_duration'] = f'{prev_step}: {elapsed:.1f}s'
341	state['prev_step_started'] = state.get('step_started', now.isoformat())
342	json.dump(state, open(f, 'w'), indent=2)
343	print(json.dumps(state, indent=2))
344	"
345	```
346	
347	---
348	
349	## Subagent Dispatch
350	
351	The main agent drives the FSM and reports progress to the user between every step. Only steps that require reasoning use subagents. Mechanical steps run commands directly.
352	
353	| Step | Execution | Why |
354	|------|-----------|-----|
355	| **select** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just shell commands (grep, build, FPGA baseline, `-E` preprocess) |
356	| **analyze** | Subagent (proposer) | Requires reading code + assembly + metrics, forming hypothesis |
357	| **implement** | Subagent | Requires code editing with domain knowledge |
358	| **verify** | Main agent runs `rebuild_single.sh` + `run_qemu_test.sh --strace`. On `COMPILE_ERROR`: single `error-fix` subagent reads error and edits source | Build + QEMU are fully scripted; only error diagnosis needs reasoning |
359	| **measure** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just `run_fpga_test.sh` |
360	| **learn** | Main agent runs commands directly | Wiki page updates + state update |
361	
362	### Subagent constraints
363	
364	When dispatching to a subagent, always include these constraints in the prompt:
365	
366	**For `analyze` subagent (proposer):**
367	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (ONLY for `grep` and `rebuild_single.sh -E`)
368	- DO NOT edit any files
369	- DO NOT run compile/archive builds, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands. `rebuild_single.sh -E` (preprocess-only) IS allowed.
370	- **MUST identify the active code path from preprocessed source**, not raw source. Run `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` and read `qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i` — only surviving `#ifdef` branches appear there. Never guess from raw source: `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` etc. are defined in internal headers, not build.sh. For dispatch functions, also cross-check with `qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump` `jal`/`jalr` targets to confirm which sub-kernel was linked.
371	- Focus on data flow, algorithm choice, tiling/blocking, loop structure, GEMM strategy, im2col, unrolling, LMUL selection, vector register utilization. Compute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the assembly. Must read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant wiki pages for what's been tried.
372	- Output: analysis summary including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, active code path (verified from preprocessed `.i`, cross-checked with objdump for dispatch cases), and 1–3 ranked ideas with expected impact rationale, risk assessment, and which lines to modify
373	
374	**For `error-fix` subagent (on `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR`):**
375	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
376	- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
377	- Input: the full gcc stderr output + the source file path of the file being optimized
378	- Read the error, identify which macro/function/type is missing or misused (often `__riscv_*` intrinsics mistakenly used in an `ENA_VEC_ISA` block), and edit the source to fix it. Same intrinsic rules as `implement` (NDS_VEC_* only, no __riscv_*).
379	- Output: description of exact code change made to fix the error
380	
381	**For `implement` subagent:**
382	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
383	- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
384	- DO NOT run build, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands
385	- DO NOT compile anything
386	- **CRITICAL: Use `NDS_VEC_*` macros ONLY** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`). NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics — they require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is NOT defined. Before writing new RVV code, read an existing working function in the same `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` block to learn the correct macro names.
387	- Output: description of exact code change made
388	
389	---
390	
391	## Rules
392	
393	1. **Fully autonomous.** Do not wait for user input at any step. Run the entire loop.
394	2. **Main agent drives the FSM.** The main agent owns state, runs mechanical steps directly, dispatches reasoning steps to subagents, and reports progress to the user between every step.
395	3. **Never skip steps.** Follow FSM transitions strictly.
396	4. **Max 5 rounds per function.** After round 5, exit to idle regardless.
397	5. **analyze is the gatekeeper.** If it finds no optimization opportunity from code + assembly + metrics, exit to idle. Do not force pointless changes.
398	6. **FPGA is ground truth.** QEMU is correctness only.
399	7. **Always record lessons.** Wins AND losses. This compounds knowledge across functions.
400	8. **Target VLEN=1024.** Never sacrifice VLEN=1024 performance.
401	9. **If verify fails 3 times**, go back to `analyze` with a different approach, not the same one.
402	10. **If a round regresses**, revert the code before trying the next approach.
403	11. **Arithmetic intensity and reuse rate drive the next round.** Recompute them each round from updated assembly to track whether changes improved memory efficiency.
404	12. **Use `rebuild_single.sh` for incremental builds** after the initial `build_libnn.sh` in `select`. Never rebuild the full library in the optimization loop.
405	13. **Use `run_fpga_test.sh` for FPGA measurement.** It handles compilation with perf flags, GDB upload, and cycle count parsing. **NEVER compile or run FPGA tests manually — always use this script.**
406	14. **Only test the target function** during the optimization loop. Full regression is for final validation only.
407	15. **Scripts are the single source of truth for builds and tests.** Never construct gcc, QEMU, or GDB commands manually. Always use the scripts in `qemu-validate/`.
408

file.content:
---
name: optimize
description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 6-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
argument-hint: [function_name|status|reset]
disable-model-invocation: false
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
---

# libnn RVV Optimization Skill

Fully autonomous optimization loop. State persisted in `.claude/opt-state.json`.

## Current State

!`LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel); cat ${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/opt-state.json 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`

## Commands

- `/optimize <function_name>` — Start optimizing a function (loops until flat land).
- `/optimize status` — Show current state
- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle

Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`

## Finite State Machine

```
idle → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
                  ↑  |         ↑          |  |                          |
                  |  |         └──────────┘  |                          |
                  |  |      (test fail → fix)|                          |
                  |  |                       └→ analyze                 |
                  |  |                (3 retries exhausted)             |
                  |  └→ idle (no opportunity found)                     |
                  |                                                     |
                  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)

Exit conditions:
  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
  - max 5 rounds per function → idle (see Rules #4)
```

The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from FPGA measurements, updated assembly, and recomputed metrics. The verify/measure loop provides real feedback.

---

## Build Scripts Reference

All scripts are at `qemu-validate/` (relative to project root). Always use `/usr/bin/bash` to invoke them. In every bash block, define: `LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)`

| Script | Purpose | Time |
|--------|---------|------|
| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files). Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
| `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` | Preprocess only (reuses the same flags). Output: `qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i`. Use in `analyze` to see which `#ifdef` branches survive. | ~0.3s |
| `run_qemu_test.sh <name> --strace --vlen 1024` | Build test binary + run on QEMU with fd-9 strace capture + parse pass/fail. Use in `verify`. | ~2s |
| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. | ~30s |

---

## State: `idle`

No optimization in progress.

If a function name is provided, transition → `select`.
If "status", report idle. If "reset", clear state.

---

## State: `select`

Validate the function and gather baseline context.

1. Find the source file under `Source/` — grep for the function name
2. Confirm it has `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` (existing RVV to improve)
3. Find corresponding test in `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`
4. Query the wiki for prior lessons and applicable techniques:
   - Read `docs/wiki/index.md` to identify relevant pages
   - Read the operator page in `docs/wiki/operators/` if it exists (e.g., `conv-hwc-s8-sym-bias-fast.md`)
   - Read matching `docs/wiki/general/` pages for applicable techniques and known anti-patterns
5. Full build (first time only):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/build_libnn.sh
   ```
6. Run baseline FPGA measurement (**MUST use this exact script — do NOT compile or run FPGA tests manually**):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
   ```
   Parse both counts from the output lines (same format as `measure` state):
   - `"The cycle count is <N>"` → `cycles_before`
   - `"The inst count is <N>"` → `instrs_before`
   (The `RESULT: PASS (cycles=N)` summary line also carries cycles but not instrs, so use the raw lines for both.)
7. **Identify the active code path.** Primary: preprocess the source with the same build flags and read the surviving `#ifdef` branches:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>
   # Output: qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i
   ```
   Secondary (for dispatch functions that call sub-kernels): confirm the linked sub-function names from the perf objdump:
   ```bash
   grep -A500 '<function_name>:' qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | \
     grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" | head -20
   ```
   Save `active_path_file` (path to `.i`) and `called_functions` in state.
8. Save state: function, source_file, test_name, cycles_before, instrs_before, active_path_file, called_functions.

**Transition:** → `analyze`
**Fail:** Function not found or no RVV → stay `select`, report error.

---

## State: `analyze`

Study the current RVV code and assembly to form the next optimization hypothesis.

**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from preprocessed source, not from raw `#ifdef` guards.**

Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from raw source alone.** Instead:

1. **Primary method — preprocessed source.** Run the file through the preprocessor with the exact build flags:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>
   # Output: qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i
   ```
   The `.i` file contains only the branches that survive preprocessing — it directly answers "which `#ifdef` block is active" without inference. Grep for the function in the `.i` file and read that body. This works even when the active block is fully inline (no sub-function calls), which objdump cannot disambiguate.

2. **Secondary method — objdump cross-check.** For functions that dispatch to sub-kernels, confirm by checking which sub-functions the linked binary actually calls:
   ```bash
   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
   ```
   Cross-reference call targets (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) with the preprocessed source. This catches cases where LTO/dead-code elimination pruned a branch that `-E` still shows.

3. When mapping the `.i` body back to raw source for editing, target only the `#ifdef` branch whose body matches the `.i`. Modifying any other branch has zero effect.

**Quick reference of known-active defines for AX45MPV** (the `.i` file is authoritative — treat this list as a hint, not ground truth):
- `ENA_VEC_ISA`, `NDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000`, `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD` — from build.sh
- `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` — unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`
- `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC`, `ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC` — typically NOT defined (confirm against `.i`)

### Analysis

**On first round (round=1):**
1. Identify the active code path from the `.i` file saved in `active_path_file` by `select`. Read the function body there to understand what will actually execute.
2. Find the function's assembly in `libnn.objdump`:
   ```bash
   grep -A 200 '<function_name>:' ${LIB_ROOT}/libnn.objdump | head -300
   ```
3. If the function calls sub-functions (GEMM kernels, etc.), run `rebuild_single.sh -E` on each sub-function's source file too, and read its assembly from `libnn.objdump`. Sub-functions have their own `#ifdef` branches.
4. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md`, relevant `general/` pages for similar patterns, operator page if it exists
5. Compute key metrics from the assembly and source:
   - **Arithmetic Intensity** = FLOPs per byte transferred from memory. Count MAC-equivalent ops (e.g., each `vqmacc` contributes `2 * vl` FLOPs for LMUL-dependent `vl`); count bytes loaded/stored per loop iteration (load instructions x element width x vl). AI = FLOPs / bytes.
   - **Reuse Rate** = FLOPs per byte loaded (exclude stores). Measures how many times each loaded byte contributes to computation before being evicted. High reuse = cache-friendly; low reuse = memory-bound.
   - Save both as `arith_intensity` and `reuse_rate` in state.
6. Identify bottleneck using the metrics: low AI -> memory-bound (improve tiling/blocking/reuse); high AI but low IPC -> compute-bound (LMUL, register pressure, loop structure)
7. Form hypothesis: what to change and expected impact

**On subsequent rounds (round>1):**
1. Re-run `rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>` to refresh the `.i` (the previous round's `implement` modified the source, and if any macros were touched the `#ifdef` resolution may have shifted). Re-read the active block.
2. Re-check `libnn.objdump` for the updated assembly of the function.
3. Recompute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the updated assembly. Compare to previous round — did the change improve reuse or reduce memory traffic?
4. Review FPGA cycle/instruction delta from last round's `measure` step to identify new bottleneck
5. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant `general/` pages for the specific bottleneck pattern
6. Form the next hypothesis based on what the metrics reveal

**Proposer output:** 1–3 ranked ideas, each with what to change, which lines, expected impact rationale, and risk assessment. Include current arithmetic intensity and reuse rate in the summary. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.

### Output and Transitions

**Output:** Print the analysis summary (including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, and hypothesis). Do NOT wait for approval — proceed immediately.

**Decision gate:** If no concrete optimization opportunity can be identified (assembly looks clean, metrics are already optimal, LMUL/tiling are already well-tuned):
- Transition to idle with final summary.
- Do NOT force an optimization that has no clear rationale.

**Transition:** → `implement` (if opportunity found) or → `idle` (if no opportunity)

---

## State: `implement`

Apply the optimization.

1. Modify ONLY the active code path identified during `analyze`. The active path was determined from the preprocessed `.i` file (with objdump cross-check for dispatch functions). **Do NOT guess from `#ifdef` guards in the raw source** — many macros (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO`) are defined in internal headers, not build.sh. Map from the `.i` body back to the corresponding raw-source line numbers when editing.
2. If the function calls sub-functions (e.g., a GEMM kernel), the optimization may need to be applied in the sub-function's source file, not the conv function's source.
3. Do NOT touch the plain C fallback or dead `#ifdef` blocks
4. Save a description of what was changed in state (`changes` field)
5. If this is round>1 and the previous round regressed, revert the regression first, then try the new approach

**CRITICAL — Intrinsic API selection:**
The active `ENA_VEC_ISA` code path uses **`NDS_VEC_*` macros** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`, `NDS_VEC_VNCLIP_WX`). These are defined in `internal/internal_vec_isa_v1_0.h`.

**NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics** (e.g., `__riscv_vle8_v_i8m2`, `__riscv_vqmacc_vx_i32m8`) in `ENA_VEC_ISA` blocks. Those require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is **NOT defined** for AX45MPV builds. Using them causes compilation failures.

When writing new RVV code, always:
- Study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef` block to learn the available `NDS_VEC_*` macro names
- Use explicit register names (`NDS_VEC_V0`, `NDS_VEC_V8`, `NDS_VEC_V16`, etc.)
- Match the LMUL register grouping: M8 uses v0/v8/v16/v24, M4 uses v0/v4/v8/.., M2 uses v0/v2/v4/..
- For loads: `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VLE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VLE32_V` (e32)
- For stores: `NDS_VEC_VSE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VSE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VSE32_V` (e32)

**Transition:** → `verify`

---

## State: `verify`

Incremental rebuild and QEMU correctness test — all via scripts.

1. Rebuild ONLY the modified libnn source (fast incremental):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh <source_file>
   ```

2. Build the test binary AND run it on QEMU with fd-9 semihosting capture in one script call:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh <test_name> --strace --vlen 1024
   ```
   The script compiles `Examples/unit_func/t_<test_name>.c` against `libnn.a` with the canonical CFLAGS, runs under `strace -e trace=write` to capture fd-9 output, and emits one of:
   - `RESULT: PASS (N sub-tests passed)` — correctness verified
   - `RESULT: FAIL (N failures detected)` — compiled but wrong results
   - `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR` — gcc failed; full error is on stderr
   - `RESULT: TIMEOUT (Ns)` — QEMU hang
   - `RESULT: UNKNOWN (...)` — no pass/fail marker in output

3. Do NOT run full regression in the optimization loop — only the target function's test.

**Pass:** → `measure`
**`COMPILE_ERROR`:** Launch ONE `error-fix` subagent with the full stderr + source file path. It reads the error and edits the source. Re-run step 1+2. Max 3 compile-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
**`FAIL` (wrong results):** → `implement` (diagnose + fix). Max 3 test-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
**`TIMEOUT` / `UNKNOWN`:** Treat as FAIL, same retry policy.

---

## State: `measure`

Run on the AX45MPV FPGA board via GDB for ground-truth cycle counts.

```bash
/usr/bin/bash ${LIB_ROOT}/qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
```

This script:
1. Compiles the test with `-DPF_COUNTER -DENA_TEST_PERF -DENA_RUN_TWICE` flags
2. Uploads binary to FPGA via GDB expect script (`riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board`)
3. Runs on AX45MPV board (`sw-boards.andestech.com:1116`)
4. Prints cycle count: `"The cycle count is NNNN"`
5. Generates `.adx` and `.objdump` in `qemu-validate/work/`

Parse the output for:
- `"The cycle count is <N>"` — the FPGA cycle count
- `"The inst count is <N>"` — the FPGA instruction count
- `"accuracy checking pass/fail"` — correctness check

Save `cycles_after` and `instrs_after` in state. Compute speedup: `cycles_before / cycles_after`. Compute IPC: `instrs_after / cycles_after`.

**Transition:** → `learn`

---

## State: `learn`

Record the lesson, then loop back.

1. Update the wiki following the wiki-writer skill:
   - Read `.claude/skills/wiki-writer/SKILL.md`
   - Input context from state: function, source_file, round, hypothesis, changes, cycles_before, cycles_after, speedup, arith_intensity, reuse_rate, outcome
   - Execute all 4 operations: operator page, general page (if applicable), index, log
   - All content must follow the naive expression style defined in wiki-writer

2. Increment the `round` counter in state.

3. **Check exit condition:**
   - If `round >= 5`, STOP → `idle`. Print final summary of all rounds.
   - Otherwise, transition → `analyze` for the next optimization round on the same function.

4. When looping back to `analyze`, key inputs for the next round are: FPGA cycle/instruction counts from `measure`, updated arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from this round, updated assembly, and wiki pages. The `analyze` step will decide whether there's still an opportunity. If not, it exits to `idle`.

**Exit → `idle`:** Print a summary table of all rounds (round#, approach, cycles, instrs, IPC, AI, reuse_rate, speedup, insight). Instruction count diagnoses whether gains came from fewer instructions (algorithmic) or better IPC (cache/scheduling).
**Continue → `analyze`:** Print "Round N+1: continuing. AI=X, reuse_rate=Y, key finding: <insight>"

---

## State File Format

```json
{
  "step": "idle|select|analyze|implement|verify|measure|learn",
  "function": "function_name",
  "source_file": "Source/.../file.c",
  "test_name": "test_name_for_qemu",
  "round": 0,
  "hypothesis": "what we plan to change and why",
  "changes": "description of what was implemented",
  "cycles_before": "",
  "cycles_after": "",
  "instrs_before": "",
  "instrs_after": "",
  "speedup": "",
  "arith_intensity": "",
  "reuse_rate": "",
  "active_path_file": "",
  "called_functions": [],
  "round_history": [
    {
      "round": 1,
      "approach": "...",
      "cycles": "...",
      "instrs": "...",
      "ipc": "...",
      "arith_intensity": "...",
      "reuse_rate": "...",
      "speedup": "...",
      "outcome": "improved|regressed|neutral",
      "insight": "..."
    }
  ],
  "history": ["timestamps of state transitions"]
}
```

Update state file on every transition:
```bash
python3 -c "
import json
from datetime import datetime
import subprocess
lib_root = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'rev-parse', '--show-toplevel']).decode().strip()
f = lib_root + '/.claude/opt-state.json'
state = json.load(open(f))
prev_step = state['step']
new_step = '<new_step>'
now = datetime.now()
state['step'] = new_step
state['step_started'] = now.isoformat()
state['history'].append(f'{now.isoformat()} {new_step}')
# Calculate time spent in previous step
if 'step_started' in state and state['history']:
    prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get('prev_step_started', now.isoformat()))
    elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
    state['last_step_duration'] = f'{prev_step}: {elapsed:.1f}s'
state['prev_step_started'] = state.get('step_started', now.isoformat())
json.dump(state, open(f, 'w'), indent=2)
print(json.dumps(state, indent=2))
"
```

---

## Subagent Dispatch

The main agent drives the FSM and reports progress to the user between every step. Only steps that require reasoning use subagents. Mechanical steps run commands directly.

| Step | Execution | Why |
|------|-----------|-----|
| **select** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just shell commands (grep, build, FPGA baseline, `-E` preprocess) |
| **analyze** | Subagent (proposer) | Requires reading code + assembly + metrics, forming hypothesis |
| **implement** | Subagent | Requires code editing with domain knowledge |
| **verify** | Main agent runs `rebuild_single.sh` + `run_qemu_test.sh --strace`. On `COMPILE_ERROR`: single `error-fix` subagent reads error and edits source | Build + QEMU are fully scripted; only error diagnosis needs reasoning |
| **measure** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just `run_fpga_test.sh` |
| **learn** | Main agent runs commands directly | Wiki page updates + state update |

### Subagent constraints

When dispatching to a subagent, always include these constraints in the prompt:

**For `analyze` subagent (proposer):**
- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (ONLY for `grep` and `rebuild_single.sh -E`)
- DO NOT edit any files
- DO NOT run compile/archive builds, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands. `rebuild_single.sh -E` (preprocess-only) IS allowed.
- **MUST identify the active code path from preprocessed source**, not raw source. Run `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` and read `qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i` — only surviving `#ifdef` branches appear there. Never guess from raw source: `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` etc. are defined in internal headers, not build.sh. For dispatch functions, also cross-check with `qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump` `jal`/`jalr` targets to confirm which sub-kernel was linked.
- Focus on data flow, algorithm choice, tiling/blocking, loop structure, GEMM strategy, im2col, unrolling, LMUL selection, vector register utilization. Compute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the assembly. Must read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant wiki pages for what's been tried.
- Output: analysis summary including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, active code path (verified from preprocessed `.i`, cross-checked with objdump for dispatch cases), and 1–3 ranked ideas with expected impact rationale, risk assessment, and which lines to modify

**For `error-fix` subagent (on `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR`):**
- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
- Input: the full gcc stderr output + the source file path of the file being optimized
- Read the error, identify which macro/function/type is missing or misused (often `__riscv_*` intrinsics mistakenly used in an `ENA_VEC_ISA` block), and edit the source to fix it. Same intrinsic rules as `implement` (NDS_VEC_* only, no __riscv_*).
- Output: description of exact code change made to fix the error

**For `implement` subagent:**
- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
- DO NOT run build, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands
- DO NOT compile anything
- **CRITICAL: Use `NDS_VEC_*` macros ONLY** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`). NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics — they require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is NOT defined. Before writing new RVV code, read an existing working function in the same `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` block to learn the correct macro names.
- Output: description of exact code change made

---

## Rules

1. **Fully autonomous.** Do not wait for user input at any step. Run the entire loop.
2. **Main agent drives the FSM.** The main agent owns state, runs mechanical steps directly, dispatches reasoning steps to subagents, and reports progress to the user between every step.
3. **Never skip steps.** Follow FSM transitions strictly.
4. **Max 5 rounds per function.** After round 5, exit to idle regardless.
5. **analyze is the gatekeeper.** If it finds no optimization opportunity from code + assembly + metrics, exit to idle. Do not force pointless changes.
6. **FPGA is ground truth.** QEMU is correctness only.
7. **Always record lessons.** Wins AND losses. This compounds knowledge across functions.
8. **Target VLEN=1024.** Never sacrifice VLEN=1024 performance.
9. **If verify fails 3 times**, go back to `analyze` with a different approach, not the same one.
10. **If a round regresses**, revert the code before trying the next approach.
11. **Arithmetic intensity and reuse rate drive the next round.** Recompute them each round from updated assembly to track whether changes improved memory efficiency.
12. **Use `rebuild_single.sh` for incremental builds** after the initial `build_libnn.sh` in `select`. Never rebuild the full library in the optimization loop.
13. **Use `run_fpga_test.sh` for FPGA measurement.** It handles compilation with perf flags, GDB upload, and cycle count parsing. **NEVER compile or run FPGA tests manually — always use this script.**
14. **Only test the target function** during the optimization loop. Full regression is for final validation only.
15. **Scripts are the single source of truth for builds and tests.** Never construct gcc, QEMU, or GDB commands manually. Always use the scripts in `qemu-validate/`.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Now the FSM diagram:

input.old_string:
```
idle → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
                  ↑  |         ↑          |  |                          |
                  |  |         └──────────┘  |                          |
                  |  |      (test fail → fix)|                          |
                  |  |                       └→ analyze                 |
                  |  |                (3 retries exhausted)             |
                  |  └→ idle (no opportunity found)                     |
                  |                                                     |
                  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)

Exit conditions:
  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
  - max 5 rounds per function → idle (see Rules #4)
```

content.text: Now the Build Scripts Reference table and path convention:

input.old_string:
## Build Scripts Reference

All scripts are at `qemu-validate/` (relative to project root). Always use `/usr/bin/bash` to invoke them. In every bash block, define: `LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)`

| Script | Purpose | Time |
|--------|---------|------|
| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files). Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
| `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` | Preprocess only (reuses the same flags). Output: `qemu-validate/work/<basename>.i`. Use in `analyze` to see which `#ifdef` branches survive. | ~0.3s |
| `run_qemu_test.sh <name> --strace --vlen 1024` | Build test binary + run on QEMU with fd-9 strace capture + parse pass/fail. Use in `verify`. | ~2s |
| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. | ~30s |
input.new_string:
## Build Scripts Reference

All scripts live under the skill itself at `.claude/skills/optimize/script/`. They each source `parse_config.sh` to pull toolchain paths, CFLAGS, QEMU/FPGA targets, and work dir from `config.toml` — no hardcoded values. Always invoke with `/usr/bin/bash`.

**Shell-variable convention for every bash block in this skill:**
```bash
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
```
When you need config values (work dir, toolchain, etc.) inside a bash block, `source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"` and use the exported `CFG_*` variables (e.g. `${CFG_WORK_DIR}`, `${CFG_CC}`, `${CFG_CPU}`).

| Script | Purpose | Time |
|--------|---------|------|
| `validate_config.py` | 7-step fail-fast config validation (toolchain, QEMU, FPGA .inc parse, FPGA TCP, GDB run). Used by `validate` state. | ~10s |
| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files) using `${CFG_CPU}`. Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c with `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE}`, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
| `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` | Preprocess only (same CFLAGS). Output: `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i`. Use in `analyze` to see which `#ifdef` branches survive. | ~0.3s |
| `run_qemu_test.sh <name>` | Build test binary + run on QEMU with fd-9 strace capture + parse pass/fail. Uses `${CFG_QEMU_*}`. Use in `verify`. | ~2s |
| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. Uses `${CFG_FPGA_*}`. Use in `select` baseline + `measure`. | ~30s |
